A strong jet stream moved across the Billings Forecast Area. Westerly winds aloft at 700mb were around 60-70kts. 850mb winds were also westerly about 40-50kts. Some of these winds aloft mixed to the surface which resulted in high wind gusts across the entire forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 70 mph at the Kilo RAWS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong westerly flow aloft along with an increasing surface pressure gradient began the initial set up for a prolonged and intense wind event. Then an approaching disturbance and attendant cold front accelerated the H700 wind speeds to the 80 to 100 kt range, which are values typically observed at much higher levels in the atmosphere. The dynamics ahead of this system along with the aforementioned surface pressure gradient and brought some of these winds down to the surface. The strong winds began over the Rocky Mountain Front before expanding into the plains and other areas. The most significant impacts from this system occurred on February 1st, when there were reports of damage to property, numerous road closures due to blowing dust, and several wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Non thunderstorm winds in Babb continued to gust over 65 kts until about 4:30 AM. This wind was strong enough to knock over a large empty ice chest at the general store.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds aloft and an environment just favorable enough to bring some of those winds to the surface combined to spread severe wind gusts downslope of the Little Rocky Mountains in southwestern Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Zortman BLM RAWS site (ALDM8), the first severe wind gust of 61 mph was recorded at 2:32 PM. The peak wind gust of 65 mph was recorded at 3:32 PM. Lesser, but still severe wind gusts continued through 4:32 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a very strong jet stream over the region, severe wind gusts were able to reach the surface off the higher elevations of southwestern Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site (MTMAL), a peak wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at 10:44 PM.  Sustained winds of 40 mph were present for only about 90 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds aloft combined with a favorable environment to allow severe wind gusts to reach the ground.  A sudden snow shower which moved through the area, was especially helpful in bringing many wind gusts in excess of 60 mph to the surface for many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the BLM Zortman East mesonet site (MTM38), wind gusts of at least 58 mph were measured multiple times from 4:15 PM through 9:30 PM.  The peak wind gust of 61 mph was recorded at 6:15 PM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Glasgow airport ASOS site (KGGW), the first severe wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at 7:53 PM.  Severe wind gusts continued for about the next hour, but there was a communications outage or some failure from the wind sensor, but NWS employees on shift were able to observe a maximum wind gust of 66 mph sometime between 8:18 PM and 8:50 PM. Times are estimated.  By the time the wind sensor came back online at 9:53 PM, the severe winds had already dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very large and very cold arctic air mass from the Canadian Rockies, swept south and east over our entire region.  With enough clearing skies, even light winds resulted in wind chill values lower than 40 below zero for many northeast Montana locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the USCRN at Poplar River site (PRSM8), 29 ENE Wolf Point, temperatures of -26 to -28 combined with wind speeds of 4 to 5 mph to create wind chill values of -40 to -42 for a period of 3 hours during the early morning of the 15th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta mesonet site, on the southern edge of Malta (A3570), temperatures of -21 to -27 combined with wind speeds of 6 to 8 mph to create wind chill values of -40 to -44 for a 15 hour period from the night of the 14th through the morning of the 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A re-enforcing cold air mass from southern Saskatchewan swept across the international border into northeast Montana.  The associated gusty winds from the west and northwest dropped wind chill values below -40 for some locations north of US Highway 2 and east of MT Highway 24.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the McDonalds MT-13 DOT site (MTMCD), temperatures of -12 to -16 combined with wind speeds of 11 to 32 mph to create wind chill values of near -40 to -43 for more than 10 hours from the night of the 17 through the early morning of the 18th.

In addition, at the Crosby RAWS site (CSBN8), temperatures of -12 to -16 combined with wind speeds of 17 to 32 mph to create wind chill values of -40 to -42 for about 7 hours through most of the morning of the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plentiful surface moisture from recently melted snow, combined with light winds overnight to allow dense freezing fog to form and persist into the morning hours of the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Culbertson mesonet site KS85, visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less for about 7 hours from the night of the 29th into the morning of the 30th, with a one hour break in the 3:00 AM hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm had a favorable combination of moisture and cold arctic air. An upper level low off the west coast extended a piece of energy into the region Friday through the weekend. This low provided ample pacific moisture and enhanced lift from an upper level warm front. Meanwhile, a cold front pushed from north to south and brought rapidly cooling temperatures along with strong��gusty��winds. The hardest hit area was north and west of a Helena��to Havre line,��where the presence of a barrier jet enabled��blizzard conditions to��develop. The end result was widespread snow for three days, with areas of blowing snow, and sub-zero temperatures once the snow ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cut Bank Airport estimates a two day total of 12 inches of snow accumulation. Snow depth was only 4 inches due to blowing and drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves embedded within a brisk west to northwest flow aloft kept strong winds going in the upper levels, which combined with a tight surface pressure gradient to bring a prolonged period of high winds over much of North-central and Southwest Montana. Some impacts from this event included a turned over semi along interstate 15 south of Shelby along with disruptions to traffic due to blowing dust north and east of Great Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A station at Two Medicine Bridge measured a 61 kts non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recent snowmelt from northern Phillips County and central and northern Valley County contributed to many area creeks and streams running freely and feeding into the main-stem Milk River. For this last week of March, most of this river flooding along the Milk River was at Tampico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milk River at Tampico gauge (TMPM8) had been transmitting erroneous data for the previous week or so due to issues with the gage. On March 31st it began recording the correct river level again after being alerted to the issue by local DES, and repairs made by USGS.  With the flood stage set at 23.0 feet, the gauge immediately showed the river at 23.4 feet at 4:13 PM MST.  Through the rest of the day to end the month of March, the river had risen to a level of 23.5 feet. This flooding continued into the first few days of April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This winter storm had a favorable combination of moisture and cold arctic air. An upper level low off the west coast extended a piece of energy into the region Friday through the weekend. This low provided ample pacific moisture and enhanced lift from an upper level warm front. Meanwhile, a cold front pushed from north to south and brought rapidly cooling temperatures along with strong��gusty��winds. The hardest hit area was north and west of a Helena��to Havre line,��where the presence of a barrier jet enabled��blizzard conditions to��develop. The end result was widespread snow for three days, with areas of blowing snow, and sub-zero temperatures once the snow ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimates 14 inches of snow accumulation at 5 SW Stanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds associated with a late-season arctic front and an impressive pressure gradient wreaked havoc across northwest Montana, especially in the Flathead Valley. According to the utility there, this was one of their worst wind events in several decades, with more damage observed than the Thanksgiving 2019 wind storm. The high winds also contributed to several ski resort closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local utility company remarked that the Red Owl area was one of the worst areas hit by the high winds. There was so much damage that it took 3 or 4 days to clear up the tree debris. They said it was difficult to mitigate during the event because there were trees falling all around them. A local said that they often get these winds coming down out of the canyon where the Red Owl Trail starts. Multiple trees had also fallen along Meadow Lake Road, Whitetail Road, and Landmark Lane downwind or west of Bear Creek Canyon. Approximately 6 trees fell across South Ferndale Road with dozens of trees down towards Highway 83. A local remarked that these were 100 year old trees that were laid down from east to west. A man was injured when a tree fell on him, near the northern part of Swan Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds associated with a late-season arctic front and an impressive pressure gradient wreaked havoc across northwest Montana, especially in the Flathead Valley. According to the utility there, this was one of their worst wind events in several decades, with more damage observed than the Thanksgiving 2019 wind storm. The high winds also contributed to several ski resort closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station FW5910, located on Lake Blaine reported multiple wind gusts to 70+ mph between 1800 and 1900MST on the 13th. Widespread wind damage was reported from along the western foothills of the Swan Range and also of the Mission Mountains. Multiple large trees fell over, and 5 power poles were snapped in half along Highway 35 from mile-markers 5, 8, 9.7 to 25. This resulted in road closure between Polson and Bigfork from 1600MST on the 13th to late morning on the 14th.  Also, multiple power outages occurred in these areas that lasted well into the weekend. In this area along the eastern shore of Flathead Lake, several trees had fallen onto rooftops, and one fell on a car. A Lutheran camp, located 4 miles SE of Lakeside, reported a dozen trees down with damage to 3 cabins, a shed, and a new deck. A personal cup anemometer situated on the roof, located 8 miles east of Rollins, measured a gust to 69 mph at 1300MST on the 13th. A reporter for the Daily Interlake reported 10 large trees down near Lake Blaine. What made this wind event more difficult is that the high winds lasted longer and occurred more frequently, which made it tough for mitigation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong west to northwest flow aloft developed over North-central Montana as a trough of low pressure moved south out of Canada. A tight surface pressure gradient helped translate some of the upper level winds to the surface. Ridge top wind gusts of 80 mph were measured, while 50 to 70 mph wind gusts were observed over the plains. Dry antecedent conditions lead to blowing dust issues north and east of Great Falls and some blowing snow occurred along the immediate eastern slopes of the Northern Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A station at 2 SE Browning measured a 62 kt non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recent snowmelt from northern Phillips County and central and northern Valley County contributed to many area creeks and streams running freely and feeding into the main-stem Milk River.  Consequently, the Milk River through most of Valley County rose up to, and exceeded flood stage for multiple locations through the first week of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive snow melt caused flooding at the river gauge on Beaver Creek near Hinsdale (BCHM8) two separate times through the first week of March. First, the gauge recorded creek levels rising above flood stage of 14.0 feet at 9:49 PM MST on March 1st. The creek crested at 15.8 feet at 3:28 PM MST on March 2nd. The creek level dropped back down below flood stage at 6:49 PM MST on March 3rd.  Second, the gauge recorded creek levels rising again above flood stage of 14.0 feet at 10:29 PM MST on March 5th.  The creek crested at 14.04 feet at 11:58 PM MST on March 5th, then crested again at 14.04 feet at 2:00 AM MST on March 6th.  The creek level finally dropped back down below flood stage to end this event at 4:15 AM MST on March 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recent snowmelt from northern Phillips County and central and northern Valley County contributed to many area creeks and streams running freely and feeding into the main-stem Milk River.  Consequently, the Milk River through most of Valley County rose up to, and exceeded flood stage for multiple locations through the first week of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive snow melt caused flooding at the river gauge on the Milk River near Glasgow (GLWM8). The gauge recorded river levels rising above the flood stage of 25.0 feet at 10:59 AM MST on March 6th. The river crested at 26.41 feet at 1:59 AM MST on March 9th. River levels dropped back down below flood stage at 10:17 AM MST on March 11th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 750 people lost power in the Essex, West Glacier and Hungry Horse areas, as a result of tree debris falling onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recent snowmelt from northern Phillips County and central and northern Valley County contributed to many area creeks and streams running freely and feeding into the main-stem Milk River and Poplar River. Consequently, the lower Milk River through eastern Valley County and the Poplar River in western Roosevelt County both rose up to, and exceeded flood stage in the second week of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive snow melt caused flooding at the river gauge on the Milk River near Nashua (NSHM8). The gauge recorded river levels rising above the flood stage of 20.0 feet at 12:16 PM MST on March 7th. The river crested at 21.75 feet at 10:00 PM MST on March 8th. River levels dropped back down below flood stage at 9:38 AM MST on March 12th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive snow melt caused flooding at the river gauge on the Poplar River near Poplar (PLRM8). The gauge recorded river levels rising above the flood stage of 16.0 feet at 9:38 PM MST on March 10th. The river crested at 16.2 feet at 11:30 PM MST on March 10th. River levels dropped back down below flood stage at 3:30 AM MST on March 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another wind event impacted western Montana and brought scattered power outages and wind gusts between 45 and 60 mph across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the wind gusts were in the 40s during this event. There were a few instances of winds gusting over 50 mph along the Continental Divide, including: 61 mph north of Avon around 1154MST, 58 mph MacDonald Pass at 0949MST, 52 mph Montana Tech in Butte at 1430MST and 51 mph at the Bert Mooney Airport south of Butte at 1451MST. No damages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds associated with a late-season arctic front and an impressive pressure gradient wreaked havoc across northwest Montana, especially in the Flathead Valley. According to the utility there, this was one of their worst wind events in several decades, with more damage observed than the Thanksgiving 2019 wind storm. The high winds also contributed to several ski resort closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 30 percent of tall spruce trees in a 20 acre swatch fell over due to the high winds at the mouth of Deep Creek Canyon, located 3 miles ENE of Fortine. Multiple trees were reported down with power outages in the following areas: Glen Lake, Grave's Creek, and Farm-to-Market Road in Whitefish. A chain-linked fence was damaged at a private residence in the Deep Creek area. Power crews were unable to mitigate initially due to the hazardous conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds associated with a late-season arctic front and an impressive pressure gradient wreaked havoc across northwest Montana, especially in the Flathead Valley. According to the utility there, this was one of their worst wind events in several decades, with more damage observed than the Thanksgiving 2019 wind storm. The high winds also contributed to several ski resort closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were damaged by high winds in Missoula proper and also in the Rattlesnake area. Just before daybreak on the 14th, a 24 inch diameter tree fell onto Rattlesnake Drive, causing damage to a vehicle and blocking part of the road. A 60 to 70 foot tree snapped off about 6 feet from the base in Greenough Park, located in the Rattlesnake area, north of Missoula. Winds were gusting up to 84 mph on top of Point 6 Mountain, north of Missoula which contributed in a local ski area closing for two days straight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful frontal passage occurred during evening hours of the 6th into the early morning hours of the 7th. Winds gusting 50 to 65 mph were common across the central areas of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 58 mph 6 miles east of Columbus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad closed upper level low moved onto the California/Oregon coast and eventually swung northeastward through Western Montana. South to southwesterly flow ahead of this system pumped in moisture and unstable air, while diffluent flow at H300 provided upper level support. This initially brought scattered severe wind gusts and hail to portions of Central and North-central MT on May 18th and 19th. As the general forcing associated with the low approached the state on the 20th, the event transitioned to a more of a moderate to heavy stratiform rain event for all areas, although some severe weather was still reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports 1.25 inch diameter hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expansive surface low pressure area and plentiful moisture shifted into eastern Montana and directed multiple and persistent severe thunderstorms from south to north through the region from mid-afternoon through then late night hours of the 20th.  One of these stronger storms produced a brief weak tornado in Valley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In an unstable post-Pacific cold front environment, prolific snow bands developed over western Montana. As a secondary cold front pushed across the Continental Divide, a particularly strong snow band developed, producing snow squalls. Radar signatures were rather impressive, including reflectivity and KDP, which gave forecasters confidence to issues snow squall warnings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a snow squall moved over I-90 east of Missoula, a motorist reported visibility reduced to 200 yards and a NWS employee reported 0.25 to 0.50 mile visibility between Clinton and Bonner. A personal weather station in Clinton recorded temperatures dropping into the 20s which contributed to roads icing up.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy snow band moving south over Evaro Hill on US-93, resulted in low visibility and slick conditions. Montana Highway Patrol alerted drivers to a stuck tractor trailer 2016MST and there were reports of slide-offs near the US-93 and I-90 junction, at 2036MST. A weather spotter at Evaro Hill, reported 3 inches of snow in just 2 hours, between 1830 and 2030MST.

Damage included: the doors blown in and part of the roof blown off of a metal barn with metal roofing scattered around for 100 yards, a cottonwood tree was snapped, the metal roof of a house was peeled back, a carport roof was completely ripped off, and an aluminum boat was blown 100 feet.  Peak winds speeds were estimated at 80 mph.  Tornado was rated an EF-0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, southeasterly low level winds ahead of a surface low resulted in dew points rising into the 50s to near 60 degrees across southeast Montana. Very cold air aloft, near -16 Celsius, combined with lift from upper level shortwaves, brought heavy snow bands to west-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a snowband moved south and east of Missoula, a NWS employee driving along I-90 near Clinton, reported 0.25 mile visibility and dangerous travel conditions. In response, a snow squall warning was issued and re-issued an additional hour at expiration. Impacts observed were slide-offs at 2030MST and a crash with injury at 2130MST. During the subsequent day, a snow report of 8 inches for the event was tweeted from Clinton, MT and a Bearmouth, MT wind sensor measured winds of 15 to 25 MPH between 2200 and 2225MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad closed upper level low moved onto the California/Oregon coast and eventually swung northeastward through Western Montana. South to southwesterly flow ahead of this system pumped in moisture and unstable air, while diffluent flow at H300 provided upper level support. This initially brought scattered severe wind gusts and hail to portions of Central and North-central MT on May 18th and 19th. As the general forcing associated with the low approached the state on the 20th, the event transitioned to a more of a moderate to heavy stratiform rain event for all areas, although some severe weather was still reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports hail up to 1.25 inches in diameter covering the ground a few inches deep in areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowband that had been steadily moving south began to back-build over south Missoula, encompassing a stretch of Highway 93 called the 's-curves', which are highly susceptible to impacts from snow accumulation. Snowfall totals were variable over short distances near the airport: areas northwest of Missoula received 3.3 inches, while 5 miles south of the airport 9 inches were observed during the 5 hour event. An NWS employee considered their commute to work in the top 10 worst winter commutes they've experienced in the area. Finally, there was a multi-car accident just north of the s-curves on Highway 93, around 0600MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front moved across the Northern Rockies during the late afternoon and early evening hours causing wintry impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of heavy snow in front of a cold front developed in Anaconda, MT, which moved towards I-90. An AWOS weather station in Anaconda measured a reduction in visibility to less than 0.25 of a mile in 30 minutes along with winds increasing from 13 mph to 25 mph gusting to 43 mph. Also there was reported whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving shortwave trough and attendant surface cold front moved through the region on Sunday May 31st. Strong southwest to west winds developed along and behind the front as it swept through the area. Some convection was observed ahead of the front but most of the wind gusts were classified as non thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cut Bank ASOS reported a 57 kt (66 mph) wind gust that was classified as non thunderstorm. Light rain was occurring at the time but thunderstorms were distant.

A Montana Department of Transportation road sensor located at MacDonald Pass, reported winds of 40 mph gusting to 57 mph, while whiteout conditions were observed via their webcam. Additionally, thundersnow was reported just to the west in Elliston, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds from a strong cold front brought trees down, power outages, damage to cars and houses, and at least two emergency travel only declarations from municipalities. Over four thousand customers were without power for up to nine hours across northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 60 mph in Hungry Horse and up to 62 mph at Marias Pass. High winds caused downed trees on US-2 between West Glacier and Marias Pass, but particularly at Nyack or at mile-marker 164. The winds also damaged power lines and transmission lines, causing power outages for approximately 9 hours. A tree fell onto a cabin 7 miles west of Marias Pass. A local ski resort, Whitefish Mountain Resort, had to close two chair lifts and a T-bar due to high winds. A rock slide occurred at mile-marker 154 on US-2 and MDT remarked that they had to remove 6 loader buckets of large boulders.

US-2 was also closed at mile-marker 89 for a period. Multiple power outages were reported from Troy to Libby and trees fell on cars and a mobile home in Libby.

Multiple trees were reported down and the media tweeted photos of a large tree that was uprooted in Kalispell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted between 58 and 60 mph between 414pm and 453pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving band of precipitation brought heavy snow to parts of southwest Montana above 5500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy, wet snowfall of up to 12 inches, fell at Georgetown Lake during the morning hours. A few power outages also occurred because of the weight of the snow on powerlines. Higher elevations adjacent to Georgetown Lake reported up to 18 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms passed through the area early Sunday, bringing a half inch to one inch of rain, which combined with ongoing snow melt to produce a near historical high discharge and river level.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overbank flooding was reported along much of the North Fork of the Flathead River, from the North Fork and Camas Road intersection, north to the Canadian border. While much of the adjacent areas are rural, flooding did occur on North Fork Road and other local roads around Polebridge through June 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rainfall of 1 to 2 inches, combined with high elevation snow melt, brought small stream flooding and river rises.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Swollen small streams brought flooding to fields and low-lying areas in the Bitterroot Valley. A few homes were surrounded by water and reported water in basements. Local and county roads, along with a small section of Eastside Highway north of Stevensville, experienced water flowing across roadways. A Forest Service campground south of Darby was also closed because of the stream flooding.  In addition, the Bitterroot River rose enough to flood ranch fields around Lolo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to heavy rainfall between one and two inches, combined with mountain snow melt, brought the Clark Fork River above Missoula over flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clark Fork River flooded the Orchard Homes neighborhood in Missoula. Neighborhood streets turned into streams, with 10 to 15 homes sandbagged and surrounded by flood water. Flooding continued through the second week of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms brought high winds and tree damage during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree damage led to power outages and blocked roadways. Damage was noted on both sides of Flathead Lake, but damage was most concentrated near Polson, Dayton, and Finley Point.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Nelson Creek Recreation Area, law enforcement officials reported three unoccupied camping trailers were rolled and destroyed.

Damaging winds from a strong cold front brought trees down, power outages, damage to cars and houses, and at least two emergency travel only declarations from municipalities. Over four thousand customers were without power for up to nine hours across northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were measured between 30 and 40 mph with wind gusts up to 75 mph during the event. The highest measured wind gust in the valley was 75 MPH, located southeast of Stevensville. Mount Sentinel, which is situated at 5000 feet MSL, recorded a gust to 96 MPH at 1330MST. Winds blew down power lines, blew over a semi-truck on US-93 in Stevensville, MT, which closed all 4 lanes of traffic and caused trees to fall over and total a truck in Stevensville. Also the Missoula County Sheriff issued a shelter in place and emergency travel only between Lolo and Stevensville because of the wind threat. Local utilities estimated an excess of 2800 customers without power. A utility remarked that this was major impact to them and stated that trees had fallen into power lines in at least 20 locations in Florence and east Carlton Creek area was the hardest hit with 50 trees down including 2 foot in diameter Ponderosa Pine to smaller Lodgepole trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms brought high winds and tree damage during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread high winds were reported as a line of thunderstorms, in the form of a squall line, moved north-northeastward across the region. While wind gusts of 50 to 60 miles per hour were common, a person from the public in Kila reported a measured peak wind gust of 78 miles per hour, and the ASOS at Glacier Park International Airport  recorded a peak wind gust of 69 miles per hour. Numerous trees were either snapped in half or uprooted,��which resulted in over 200 power outages and 37,000 customers without power. Several houses and vehicles were destroyed as trees fell onto them. Highway 2 and Montana Highway 83 were both covered by fallen trees for a time. A local electric coop remarked that this wind event may be the largest, most damaging outage event in our history.

A compact upper level closed low moved southeastward through Montana during the early morning hours of Thursday May 7th. Most lower elevation locations reported moderate to heavy rainfall with this system, though the combination H700 temperatures around -5C and diabatic cooling brought a period of accumulating snow to Fergus and Judith Basin County locations at and below 4000 ft. The greatest impacts were exerted on area power infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall estimated from 8 inch snow depth and after the snow ended. Some melting and compaction was taken in account.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outages were reported near Geyser and Judith Gap due to heavy wet snow weighing transmission lines down. Report was relayed by Fergus Electric Cooperative via facebook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds from a strong cold front brought trees down, power outages, damage to cars and houses, and at least two emergency travel only declarations from municipalities. Over four thousand customers were without power for up to nine hours across northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted from 53 to 58 mph from Georgetown Lake to Deer Lodge and Avon. These winds resulted in part of the roof of the Deer Lodge Hotel being blown off, multiple power outages, and trailers flipped on I-90 around Butte, MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds from a strong cold front brought trees down, power outages, damage to cars and houses, and at least two emergency travel only declarations from municipalities. Over four thousand customers were without power for up to nine hours across northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts over 50 mph near Clinton, contributed to power outages to an estimated 800 customers from Bonner to Helmville. An area ski resort had to close down due to the high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front moved across the Northern Rockies during the late afternoon and early evening hours causing wintry impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Taking into account previous snow squall reports, as the front advanced eastward towards Butte, MT., a snow squall warning was issued for a radar reflectivity of 30 to 35 dBZ with impressive KDP. The ASOS at the Bert Mooney Airport, south of Butte, reported heavy snow and winds 24 mph gusting to 49 mph. The visibility sensor at the airport was not working at that time. The Montana Department of Transportation hoisted severe driving conditions at Homestake Pass during this event.

Strong winds from aloft were departing the region, but were able to remain over the Dawson/Wibaux County area just long enough with a favorable atmosphere that brought those winds down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site (MTBVR), sustained winds of at least 40 mph began at 8:43 AM MST and continued at least each hour until 2:53 PM MST.  Peak wind gusts of 60 mph were measured from 12:08 PM to 12:14 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and strong low pressure system over the region, combined with warm, moist air and an unstable atmosphere to form strong thunderstorms over central and southern Montana - one of which became severe as it moved into Petroleum County during the overnight and early morning hours of the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Petroleum County Emergency Manager reported hail stones up to the size of golf balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and cold air mass from the Canadian Rockies descended over the US northern Rockies and stalled over the region.  Enough moisture was drawn into the system from the Pacific to direct decent snowfall over portions of central and eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports from central and northern portions of Garfield County ranged from 6 to 8 inches. The greatest amount of 8 inches was reported from 17N Van Norman.  Times are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter 9.5 NW Carlyle reported 7 inches of new snow.  Times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recent snowmelt from northern Phillips County and central and northern Valley County contributed to many area creeks and streams running freely and feeding into the main-stem Milk River. For this last week of March, most of this river flooding along the Milk River at Tampico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continuing from the end of March, the Milk River at Tampico (TMPM8), with a flood stage at 23.0 feet, began the first day of April at 23.5 feet. The river crested at 23.6 feet on April 1st from 7:29 AM MST to 9:45 AM MST, then fell back down below flood stage on April 2nd at 2:56 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving low pressure system from Saskatchewan with strong winds aloft, combined with a favorable atmosphere to allow most of those strong winds to reach down to the surface, especially across far eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the mesonet site 5 ESE Hoyt (MTM28), a wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at 2:30 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving low pressure system from Saskatchewan with strong winds aloft, combined with a favorable atmosphere to allow most of those strong winds to reach down to the surface, especially across far eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site (MTBVR), thunderstorm wind gusts of 80 mph were measured from 2:18 PM to 2:24 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving low pressure system from Saskatchewan with strong winds aloft, combined with a favorable atmosphere to allow most of those strong winds to reach down to the surface, especially across far eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site (MTBVR), on the Dawson/Wibaux County line, sustained winds of at least 40 mph each hour, began at 9:08 AM MST, and ended at 3:59 PM MST.  Wind gusts in excess of 57 mph also occurred multiple times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the Billings Forecast Area. Behind the front, a strong disturbance within an unstable northwest flow aloft moved across the forecast area which resulted in heavy snow across most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 7 inches were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the Billings Forecast Area. Behind the front, a strong disturbance within an unstable northwest flow aloft moved across the forecast area which resulted in heavy snow across most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A spring snow storm impacted areas along the Continental Divide in west-central Montana April 10th and 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall and blowing snow was observed on U.S. Highway 12 and Montana Highway 141. Travel was difficult on Highway 12 and Montana Highway 141 as 6 to 10 inches of snow fell. Lesser snow amounts were noted on I-90 from Garrison Junction to Butte. Wind gusts up to 30 mph led to blowing snow and reduced visibility for travelers going over MacDonald Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A spring snow storm impacted areas along the Continental Divide in west-central Montana April 10th and 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snowfall and gusty easterly winds impacted travel on U.S. Highway 2 over Marias Pass the evening of April 10 through the morning of April 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A spring snow storm impacted areas along the Continental Divide in west-central Montana April 10th and 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the Seeley-Swan and Potomac valleys and along the I-90 corridor east of Missoula. Little travel impact was noted on Montana Highway 200 and 83 and along I-90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A spring snow storm impacted areas along the Continental Divide in west-central Montana April 10th and 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty north and east winds were felt across the Flathead Valley and Flathead Lake with gusts over 40 mph being observed. A downed tree damaged power lines on Echo Lake Road northeast of Bigfork. Flathead Electric noted 400 customers lost power during this strong northeast wind event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds increase significantly with the passage of a weak shortwave trough. Expect increasing mid level westerly winds into the 40 to 45 kt range. The associated cold front, translates winds down to the surface. Winds of 25 to 35 mph are common over the plains with gusts up to 40 mph, and with gusts in excess of 50 mph along The Rocky Mountain Front. Expect slightly lesser winds of 15 to 25 mph likely in the southwest valleys with gusts to 35 mph. These winds help push warm temperatures up to 10 degrees above normal. The cold front causes scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop over western Montana and to spread over central and southwest Montana (mainly along and south of a Great Falls to Lewistown line).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station dw0975 kevin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two people died after a crash involving a school bus and a car on U.S. Highway 212 between Lame Deer and Ashland. The crash occurred about 6:46 a.m. on U.S. Highway 212 at about mile marker 51. The car was traveling east on Highway 212, on a downhill slope curving right when it crossed over the center line and collided with a school bus that was driving west. The roads were icy and speed on the part of the car was considered to be a factor in the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and wind loading on weak snow layers New Year's Eve day led to an unstable snowpack to begin the New Year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three snowmobilers were involved in an avalanche approximately 1 mile northwest of Lake Dinah, or about 12 miles northwest of Seeley Lake. The riders were caught and carried a short distance; two were fully buried, and one extricated himself after being separated from his snowmobile. The dimensions of the avalanche were 560 feet long, 100 feet wide, and an average of 6 feet deep. The two riders who were fully buried could not be saved, thus two fatalities occurred as a result of the avalanche.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong winds to Northwest Montana particularly in the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts to 50 miles per hour in the valleys and to 70 miles per hour on the terrain were observed. Several areas experienced power outages including a stretch of US Highway 2 from West Glacier to Essex, as well as in the town of Hungry Horse, and the south end of Swan Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific system brought strong 700 mb winds and helped develop a lee side trough, resulting in high winds across much of the region.  The strong winds initially developed over the Rocky Mountain Front on January 3rd but then spread over the plains and portions of southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDOT station at 2 SE Browning reported a non thunderstorm wind gust of 55 kts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very large and very cold arctic air mass from the Canadian Rockies from the day before lingered over entire region. With plenty of clearing skies, gusty winds easily resulted in wind chill values lower than 40 below zero for many northeast Montana locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the US-2 @Stateline DOT site (MTSTA), temperatures of -12 to -20 combined with wind speeds of 7 to 30 mph to create wind chill values of near -40 to -45 for about 8 hours from the late night of the 15th through the early morning of the 16th.

In addition, at the Poplar RAWS site (POPM8), temperatures of -16 to -23 combined with wind speeds of 9 to 27 mph to create wind chill values of -40 to -46 for about 10 hours from the evening of the 15th through the early morning of the 16th. Also, at the McDonalds MT-13 DOT site (MTMCD), temperatures of -14 to -21 combined with wind speeds of 9 to 26 mph to create wind chill values of -40 to -46 for almost 6 hours from the late night of the 15th through the early morning of the 16th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Glentana mesonet site (F2113), temperatures of -16 to -24 combined with wind speeds of 6 to 21 mph to create wind chill values of -40 to -45 for more than 8 hours from the night of the 15th through the early morning of the 16th. In addition, at the Bluff Creek RAWS site (BLUM8), temperatures of -22 to -25 combined with wind speeds of 9 to 14 mph to create wind chill values of -45 to -47 for more about 5 hours on the night of the 15th. Also at the Honrud mesonet site (E5606), temperatures of -17 to -19 combined with winds speeds of 8 to 23 mph to create wind chill values of near -40 to -42 for about 2 hours in the early morning of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in parts of northwest Montana the evening of the 6th through much of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 12 to 18 inches were common on US Highway 2 between West Glacier and Marias Pass. Travel was difficult to impossible at times. Also, several hundred people were without power up to four hours in West Glacier as heavy snow caused power lines to snap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in parts of northwest Montana the evening of the 6th through much of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals ranged from 12 to 18 inches from Potomac to Seeley Lake. Travel was very difficult at times, especially on the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in parts of northwest Montana the evening of the 6th through much of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous travel due to heavy snow occurred on Interstate 90, leading to multiple semi truck slide offs and lane blockages near Lookout Pass. Peak 24 hour snowfall was 21 inches at Lookout Pass ending the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific system brought strong 700 mb winds and helped develop a lee side trough, resulting in high winds across much of the region. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cut Bank Airport reports a non thunderstorm wind gust of 66 kts and a sustained wind of 48 kts from 745 to 845 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds aloft and an environment just favorable enough to bring some of those winds to the surface combined to spread severe wind gusts downslope of the Little Rocky Mountains in southwestern Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site (MTMAL), sustained winds of at least 40 mph began at 1:33 PM, and persisted at least each hour through the rest of the afternoon.  The first severe wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at 3:24 PM.  The peak wind gusts of 62 mph were recorded from 3:30 PM through 3:34 PM.  Sustained winds of at least 40 mph each hour ended after 5:04 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very large and very cold arctic air mass from the Canadian Rockies, swept south and east over our entire region.  With enough clearing skies, even light winds resulted in wind chill values lower than 40 below zero for many northeast Montana locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Glentana mesonet site (F2113), temperatures of -20 to -23 combined with wind speeds of 6 to 12 mph to create wind chill values of -40 to -41 for a period of 2 hours and 45 minutes during the morning of the 15th.  In addition, at the Bluff Creek RAWS site (BLUM8), temperatures of -23 to -26 combined with wind speeds of 6 to 9 mph to create wind chill values of -41 to -43 for about 5 hours from the night of the 14th into the early morning of the 15th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Comertown Turn-Off MT-5 DOT site (MTCOM), temperatures of -15 to -24 combined with wind speeds of 6 to 20 mph to create wind chill values of -40 to -47 for almost 7 hours through the early morning of 16th. In addition, at the Plentywood mesonet site (GGWPW), temperatures of -14 to -18 combined with wind speeds of 17 to 20 mph to create wind chill values of -40 to -44 for about 3 hours during the early morning of the 16th. Also, at the Medicine Lake RAWS site (MLKM8), temperatures of -17 to -22 combined with wind speeds of 10 to 19 mph to create wind chill values of -40 to -43 for about 4 hours in the early morning of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly departing low cloud deck trapped low-level moisture near the surface, as fog easily developed and persisted along and near the eastern edge of northern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Wolf Point airport ASOS site KOLF, visibility dropped to a quarter mile for 5 hours from the evening of the 23rd into the very early morning of the 24th.  In addition, at the Poplar mesonet site (KPO1), visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less for more than 8 hours from the evening of the 23rd into the early morning of the 24th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Beach ASOS site K20U, just east of the Wibaux county line, visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less for 8 hours from the evening of the 23rd into the early morning of the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and arctic air coming over the Continental Divide led to dangerous winter travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Easterly winds gusting to 35 miles per hour and heavy snow of 10 to 18 inches resulted in periods of severe driving conditions on US Highway 2. In addition, wind chills dropped to -30 degrees at Marias Pass.  Snow bands led to isolated areas receiving nearly 3 feet of snow on North Fork Road between Columbia Falls and Polebridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and arctic air coming over the Continental Divide led to dangerous winter travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy snow led to severe driving conditions on Interstate 90 from Saint Regis to Lookout Pass. Several public schools were forced to operate on a delayed schedule the morning of the 13th as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds aloft combined with a favorable environment to allow severe wind gusts to reach the ground.  A sudden snow shower which moved through the area, was especially helpful in bringing many wind gusts in excess of 60 mph to the surface for many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the South Sawmill Creek RAWS site (SSMM8), the peak wind gust of 64 mph was recorded at 7:08 PM, and a 62 mph wind gust was recorded at 9:08 PM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Comertown Turn-Off MT-5 DOT site (MTCOM), sustained wind speeds of at least 40 mph were recorded at least each hour for about 3 1/2 hours during the early afternoon.

While some snow fell over the plains, the most impactful weather most remained over mountain areas. Gusty winds were also reported in some areas, especially along the Rocky Mountain Front but gusts remained under High Wind criteria for their respective areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and cold air mass from the Canadian Rockies descended over the US northern Rockies and stalled over the region.  Enough moisture was drawn into the system from the Pacific to direct decent snowfall over portions of central and eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter in Flatwillow reported 6 inches of new snow.  Times are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought snow across the entire forecast area with heavy snow at a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 5 to 9 inches were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow occurred along the foothills of the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts around 6 inches occurred across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts around 6 inches, along with snow packed roads, occurred across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing shortwave brought light snow to Southwest Montana while a strong surface pressure gradient between high pressure near the Idaho border and low pressure further north.  This resulted in strong southerly winds along with periods of whiteout conditions that resulted in road closures along I15 in Beaverhead County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDOT closes I15 from Idaho border to Clark Canyon Dam due to severe driving conditions from snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cold-air advection resulted in high wind gusts across Fallon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted 58 to 63 mph across the Baker area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the Billings Forecast Area. Behind the front, a strong disturbance within an unstable northwest flow aloft moved across the forecast area which resulted in heavy snow across most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 7 inches were common across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of single digit and sub-zero temperatures caused large areas of anchor ice developed on the Spring Creek in the northeastern section of Choteau, which caused up to a couple of feet flood water to enter parts of a nearby residential area and a section of railroad. The impacts from the overland flooding were mostly confined to vacant lots. However, there were reports of basement flooding in up to 80 residential houses within the city. It wasn���t clear how much of a role the flood water from the Spring Creek played in this as a rising water table also contributed to the basement flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam along the Spring Creek on the northeastern side of Choteau sent flood waters up to a couple feet in depth into a small section of the city with minor impacts. The flooding may have also played a partial role in the basement flooding reported by up to 80 houses in the city. Information was provided by the city of Choteau Facebook and by NWS field inspection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds increase significantly with the passage of a weak shortwave trough. Expect increasing mid level westerly winds into the 40 to 45 kt range. The associated cold front, translates winds down to the surface. Winds of 25 to 35 mph are common over the plains with gusts up to 40 mph, and with gusts in excess of 50 mph along The Rocky Mountain Front. Expect slightly lesser winds of 15 to 25 mph likely in the southwest valleys with gusts to 35 mph. These winds help push warm temperatures up to 10 degrees above normal. The cold front causes scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop over western Montana and to spread over central and southwest Montana (mainly along and south of a Great Falls to Lewistown line).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from mping: 1-inch tree limbs broken; shingles blown off. Wind speed and gust estimate not reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours of the 5th and into the early morning hours of the 6th, a strong and active low pressure system combined with warm, moist southeasterly winds at the surface, and a strong jet streak in the mid-levels of the atmosphere, to form strong to severe thunderstorms over central Montana. As these storms moved over northeast Montana, conditions were favorable to allow strong downdrafts to spread out many miles from the parent storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter, at 2 WSW Larslan, reported quarter size hail initially for a few minutes, which then diminished to dime size about 10 minutes later. Wind gusts up to 52 mph were also measured.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Saint Marie MARCO mesonet site, 07MT, measured a 77 mph wind gust at 2:20 AM local time, then a 65 mph wind gust 10 minutes later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system moving slowly over the region pulled in very gusty surface winds from the east and southeast ahead of the main circulation.  Any passing rain shower provided an easy path for stronger winds aloft to be pulled down to the surface, especially within 20 to 30 miles of the Montana-North Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public measured sustained winds of 40 mph and a 63 mph wind gust at their home weather station, 1 N Plentywood.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Comertown Turn-Off MT-5 DOT site (MTCOM), sustained winds of at least 40 mph were recorded at least each hour from late morning, into the mid-day hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure at the surface combined with seasonally hot temperatures, an unstable atmosphere, and enough shear to generate and sustain strong thunderstorms across portions of eastern Montana - a few of which produced severe wind gusts in Valley and Dawson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the King Coulee RAWS site (KIGM8), a 58 mph wind gust was recorded at 5:30 PM MST. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system from the Pacific Northwest and the Northern Rocky Mountains combined with and area of surface low pressure and a cold front to allow strong thunderstorms to develop amid a moderately unstable atmosphere.  One of these storms became severe as it passed through Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported, via Facebook, that some fences were blown away by strong/severe winds. Wind speed estimated. The nearby Saco US-2 DOT sensor reported a wind gust of 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure with an associated warm front and cold front, a highly unstable atmosphere, and strong wind shear all combined to form and sustain multiple severe thunderstorms which moved through eastern Montana during the afternoon and evening of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported, via Facebook, that 3.50 inches of rain fell in 90 minutes, at 4 N Raymond.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter size hail, along with measurements of 49 mph wind gusts and 0.60 inch of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Satellite and radar indicate that the bulk of moisture associated with this weather system moved through the forecast area; however, enough moisture upstream continues likely categorical PoPs for much of the CWA. Exception is the northwest plains zones, including Glacier, Toole, Liberty, and eastern portions of Pondera and Teton Counties where|only isolated/scattered light snow expected during the morning hours. In that region, Winter Weather Advisory cancelled, since very few observations are indicate any snowfall.|Remainder of the winter highlights active through noon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow measured at Grass Range 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm affects the region. Forecasts remain, generally, on track. Heaviest snow accumulation remains focused on the east slopes of The Rockies and along and to the north of The Little Belts. Total accumulations of 1 foot are forecast across areas of western Teton, NW Lewis and Clark, Judith Basin and Fergus counties near the mountains, where impacts to travel, agricultural and outdoor activities are greatest. Initial shift to northwesterly winds across the northern half of the forecast area with cooling behind the initial frontal feature a bit less aggressive than anticipated. Temperatures cooling to the 40s across the northern tier of the forecast area and to the 30s upstream over Alberta, and cooler temperatures gradually spread south into the |area. Onset of precipitation is somewhat nebulous with showers increasing through early evening before a more widespread surge of moisture and colder air moves south. Timing for heaviest precipitation across central MT shifted a bit later. While the arrival of snow and colder temperatures is somewhat slower, overall precipitation |amounts change little. Temperatures fall into the 20s for most areas north of I90, and locally high snowfall rates lead to icy or at least snow and slush covered roadways. Northerly to easterly winds increase with the arrival of colder air, creating areas of blowing and drifting snow in exposed areas. Steady or falling temperatures possibly reach as cold as the single digits in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow measured 15 inches at 7 SW Helena. Previous measurement 4 inches at 9 AM. 11 inches of snow fell in 10 hours and 47 min.

Timing for heaviest precipitation across central MT shifted a bit later. While the arrival of snow and colder temperatures is somewhat slower, overall precipitation |amounts change little. Temperatures fall into the 20s for most areas north of I90, and locally high snowfall rates lead to icy or at least snow and slush covered roadways. Northerly to easterly winds increase with the arrival of colder air, creating areas of blowing and drifting snow in exposed areas. Steady or falling temperatures possibly reach as cold as the single digits in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow 7 inches at 11 SSE Ulm. Measurements ranged from 6 to 8 inches due to blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Satellite imagery reveals a long plume of moisture streaming over a broad eastern Pacific ridge, heading toward the northern Rockies. This moisture passes over the region. A strong northwesterly flow, results in a prolonged period of heavy mountain snowfall for the Central and Southwest Mountains. Mountain snowfall occurs over the Big Belts, Bridger and Madison ranges; this expands in coverage and intensity overnight. One to three feet of new snow expected. There are some nuances to address for lower elevation impacts. As the upper level jet noses over the Northern Rockies, there will be some warming as the ridge drifts further east. Short term model|guidance supports a period of more widespread snow developing in association with this warm front. Thus, expect a trace to a few inches of snow accumulation for the southwest valleys. With rising snow levels and a break in precipitation Thursday afternoon, melting occurs on these interstate corridors. Thus, snowfall totals are below warning criteria, but note that icing could occur Thursday night with additional new snowfall enhancing the icing hazard. NBM temperatures appear to be biased a few degrees too warm for the southwest valleys, so blended towards the lower side of guidance. Following this possible break in snowfall rates, the next embedded shortwave moves through Thursday night into early Friday. This produces a period of moderate to heavy snowfall. Snow expands into portion of North-central Montana as a cold front trails behind this wave.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported 24 inches last 24 hours. 3 inches new overnight. ski resort web report. 12 nne Bozeman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-upper level shortwave trough crests the eastern Pacific ridge and dives SE across BC before shearing out across MT later in the day. Winds are quite strong along the immediate east slopes of The Rocky Mtn Front and will further increase across all areas as surface troughing deepens to the east of The Rockies while decreasing stability also brings moderate westerly flow aloft downward. Potential for wind gusts in excess of 50 kts exists along the immediate east slopes as well as some of the ridgetops across central MT, while most areas see gusts in the 35-45 kt range across the north-central MT plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured 63 mph wind gust about 5 miles southeast of browning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad upper level ridge brings clear and dry conditions. Temperatures begin to warm for most areas, although some areas along the Hi-Line and southwest valleys with continued snow cover may not warm as much as they could without snow cover. The mentioned ridge then flattens for Friday. This could bring some breezy to perhaps windy conditions, along with continued warm temperatures, and dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station mtbrn Two Medicine Bridge (mt dot). 1 ene East Glacier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions persist Saturday night with gusty west to southwest winds across the plains continuing into the evening. A weak surface front pushing south through southern Alberta during the afternoon hours moves south through north-central MT during the evening hours with the most notable affect of its passage being a wind shift to the north then lighter easterly winds by Sunday morning. A fairly vigorous shortwave trough near 140W Saturday afternoon reaches the WA/BC coast Sunday morning and moves quickly east across the Northern Rockies and MT late Sunday afternoon and night with an associated Pacific frontal system at the surface. Several |winter weather and wind hazards are expected in association with the frontal passage Sunday evening, beginning with the development of snow along the higher elevations of the Rocky Mtn Front by later Sunday afternoon. Showers will likely spread east and intensify along the frontal boundary late Sunday afternoon and evening, especially across SW MT. While temperatures are initially mild ahead of the front through Sunday afternoon, conditions appear favorable for the development of a brief but intense precipitation band along the front across southwest MT Sunday evening. With temperatures falling and precipitation changing to snow following the frontal passage. Main concern for lower elevations of SW MT is the quickly changing visibility and |potential for icing as wet surfaces freeze. As the front moves quickly E/SE through central and SW MT Sunday evening, gusty west winds likely develop along and following the frontal passage over much of central and SW MT as strong westerly flow |aloft mixes down behind the frontal passage. Flow aloft shifts to northwesterly following the initial trough passage Sunday night while low pressure at the surfaces shifts from Southern Alberta southeastward to ND, keeping fairly windy conditions in place across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station mtbrn two medicine bridge (mt dot). 1 ene East Glacier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front pushes into the region from southern Alberta, bringing a few snow showers to parts of northern and central Montana. Temperatures expected to be roughly 20-30 degrees colder than previous day. Snow widespread across SW MT through much of the day. General snow total accumulation of 3-6 inches expected for areas along and south of I90 with up to 12 inches possible in the higher elevations of the Madison and Gallatin ranges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured 8 inches snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions persist Saturday night with gusty west to southwest winds across the plains continuing into the evening. A weak surface front pushing south through southern Alberta during the afternoon hours moves south through north-central MT during the evening hours with the most notable affect of its passage being a wind shift to the north then lighter easterly winds by Sunday morning. A fairly vigorous shortwave trough near 140W Saturday afternoon reaches the WA/BC coast Sunday morning and moves quickly east across the Northern Rockies and MT late Sunday afternoon and night with an associated Pacific frontal system at the surface. Several |winter weather and wind hazards are expected in association with the frontal passage Sunday evening, beginning with the development of snow along the higher elevations of the Rocky Mtn Front by later Sunday afternoon. Showers will likely spread east and intensify along the frontal boundary late Sunday afternoon and evening, especially across SW MT. While temperatures are initially mild ahead of the front through Sunday afternoon, conditions appear favorable for the development of a brief but intense precipitation band along the front across southwest MT Sunday evening. With temperatures falling and precipitation changing to snow following the frontal passage. Main concern for lower elevations of SW MT is the quickly changing visibility and |potential for icing as wet surfaces freeze. As the front moves quickly E/SE through central and SW MT Sunday evening, gusty west winds likely develop along and following the frontal passage over much of central and SW MT as strong westerly flow |aloft mixes down behind the frontal passage. Flow aloft shifts to northwesterly following the initial trough passage Sunday night while low pressure at the surfaces shifts from Southern Alberta southeastward to ND, keeping fairly windy conditions in place across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station mtbhp Big Hole Pass (mt dot). 6 se Jackson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Satellite imagery reveals a long plume of moisture streaming over a broad eastern Pacific ridge, heading toward the northern Rockies. This moisture passes over the region. A strong northwesterly flow, results in a prolonged period of heavy mountain snowfall for the Central and Southwest Mountains. Mountain snowfall occurs over the Big Belts, Bridger and Madison ranges; this expands in coverage and intensity overnight. One to three feet of new snow expected. There are some nuances to address for lower elevation impacts. As the upper level jet noses over the Northern Rockies, there will be some warming as the ridge drifts further east. Short term model|guidance supports a period of more widespread snow developing in association with this warm front. Thus, expect a trace to a few inches of snow accumulation for the southwest valleys. With rising snow levels and a break in precipitation Thursday afternoon, melting occurs on these interstate corridors. Thus, snowfall totals are below warning criteria, but note that icing could occur Thursday night with additional new snowfall enhancing the icing hazard. NBM temperatures appear to be biased a few degrees too warm for the southwest valleys, so blended towards the lower side of guidance. Following this possible break in snowfall rates, the next embedded shortwave moves through Thursday night into early Friday. This produces a period of moderate to heavy snowfall. Snow expands into portion of North-central Montana as a cold front trails behind this wave.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported 26 inches new snow last 24 hours. 14 inches new overnight. ski area web report. 12 nne Bozeman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breezy morning winds, clear skies, a fresh snow cover, and a cold arctic air mass all combine to create dangerous wind chills across a few locations in northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the McDonalds MT-13 DOT site (MTMCD), temperatures of -16 to -20 combined with wind speeds of 10 to 13 mph to create wind chill values of near -40 to -42 for 4 and 1/2 hours in the early morning of the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong low pressure system from the Canadian Rockies and its associated cold front, produced convective snow showers and wind gusts up to 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site (MTMAL), sustained winds of 40 mph or greater were brief and isolated, but peak wind gusts of 60 mph were recorded from 2:26 PM to 2:30 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary push of colder air surges south across N-central MT during the early morning hours in association with a strong area of surface high pressure moving south from AB/SK. This should be the final but strongest surge of northerly winds southward across the forecast area with gusts to 30kts across much of north-central MT for several hrs, spreading south into SW MT where gusts to 25 kts will funnel southwards through the valleys. Snow remains most persistent over the central MT Mtns where several additional inches of snowfall are expected before precipitation diminishes with enhanced areas of snowfall during the morning, also likely near the Elkhorn, Tobacco Root and Gallatin Ranges of SW MT. Snow showers or flurries are likely through the morning over areas north of Great Falls. Expect very poor conditions from areas just east of Great Falls to Lewistown and south to the Little Belts and Big Snowy mountain ranges, where snowfall will be most persistent and gusty winds will continue to create significant blowing and drifting snow through the morning before winds and snow decrease. Temperatures fall through the morning as the cold airmass slides south into eastern MT and temperatures become steady in the 20s for many locations and only in the teens for Blaine and Hill counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured 28 inches new snow last 24 hrs. ski area web report. 7 sse Neihart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface pressure falls along The Rocky Mountain Front produce increasing west-southwesterly winds. These stronger winds produce areas of blowing snow, observed on web cams at East Glacier Park, but likely affecting other nearby areas. Expect these stronger winds to continue spreading across much of North-central Montana overnight into Friday. While maintaining fairly mild temperatures, the increasing winds also support the continuing potential for drifting or blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station mtbrn two medicine bridge (mt dot). 1 ene East Glacier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds from the mid-levels of the atmosphere spread across the region, and were able to reach down to the surface for higher elevation locations of central Montana and points just downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site (MTMAL), high winds came in three separate, distinct periods. The first one began at 1:31 AM, had a peak wind gust of 63 mph at 1:45 AM, and ended at 3:30 AM.  The second one began at 8:45 AM, had a peak wind gust of 85 mph at 10:55 AM, and ended at 2:46 PM.  The third one began at 3:57 PM, had a peak wind gust of 65 mph at 5:37 PM, and ended at 9:30 PM.  In addition, at the Zortman BLM RAWS site (ALDM8), the peak wind gust of 61 mph was recorded at 2:32 AM, and again at 9:32 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very similar set-up was in place on the 7th as was the case on the 6th. |An upper low was situated over the Pacific Northwest. This resulted in a southwest flow aloft across the Billings Forecast Area. This pattern was quite favorable for the development of severe thunderstorms. A strong southeast, surface flow advected 50+ degree dewpoints from the central Plains northwestward across the entire Billings Forecast Area. In addition, a strong jet stream overhead provided plenty of speed shear over the area. This all resulted in a few supercell thunderstorms which then evolved into a squall line.

Southwesterly flow and associated shortwave energy streaming ahead of a Pacific Northwest trough brought thunderstorms with heavy rain and gusty winds to Southwest and portions of Central/North-central Montana on Thursday July 23rd. Abundant moisture was transported into state as precipitable water values exceeded the one inch mark at times. This helped push MLCAPE values up to 1000 j/kg, while the aforementioned shortwave energy provided 25 to 35 kts of bulk shear. Storms first moved into the southwest during the late morning and early afternoon hours, while northern areas were impacted later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT Bozeman Pass station measured a 53 kt (61 mph) thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 50 kt jet streak traversing the northeast side of a ridge of high pressure combined with moisture and instability to produce isolated thunderstorms over North-central Montana. The storms over Liberty County quickly strengthened to severe levels as precipitable water values approached the one inch mark, MLCAPE topped 1000 j/kg, and bulk wind shear fell in the 25 to 35 kt range. This resulted in ping pong ball to lime sized hail, gusty winds, and heavy rain in the Lothair area. Broken windows and crop/tree damage were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported ping pong ball (1.5 inch diameter) to lime (2 inch diameter) size hail and gusty winds in excess of 50 mph. Heavy rain also caused some flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An enhanced microburst, around the intersection of 297 and Bercail Road , with winds estimated at 70-75 mph, resulted in a swath about 2 to 3 miles of wind driven rain and hail that sheared grasses and crops. Significant crop damage occurred with grass and wheat fields sheared to the ground. In addition, numerous, large cottonwood limbs with leaves stripped were downed. A small, elevated silo was blown over and minor roof damage occurred to a couple of structures. Highway 287 had pea size to 1/2 inch size gravel blown on it from the shoulder.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle disturbance moving from southern Canada through Montana combined with a moderately unstable atmosphere and just enough warm, moist air to set of one severe thunderstorm over the Little Rocky Mountains of SW Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site (MTMAL), a 62 mph wind gust was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significantly unstable atmosphere and favorable winds produced many strong thunderstorms across eastern Montana, on of which became severe over Sheridan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported hail up to the size of golf balls, as well as 0.75 inch of rain.  Time is estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very similar set-up was in place on the 7th as was the case on the 6th. |An upper low was situated over the Pacific Northwest. This resulted in a southwest flow aloft across the Billings Forecast Area. This pattern was quite favorable for the development of severe thunderstorms. A strong southeast, surface flow advected 50+ degree dewpoints from the central Plains northwestward across the entire Billings Forecast Area. In addition, a strong jet stream overhead provided plenty of speed shear over the area. This all resulted in a few supercell thunderstorms which then evolved into a squall line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low was situated over the Pacific Northwest on the 6th and 7th. This resulted in a southwest flow aloft across the Billings Forecast Area. This pattern is quite favorable for the development of severe thunderstorms. At the same time, a surface boundary stretched across northeast Wyoming northward through the southern portions of south central and southeast Montana. Dewpoints across this area were in the 50s. This, combined with strong daytime heating, resulted in CAPES around 1500j/kg. Midlevel flow was around 50kts which providing plenty of shear for rotating storms. This all resulted in the development of severe thunderstorms which resulted in large hail and some property damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable wind pattern, both aloft and at the surface, combined with a very unstable atmosphere to form strong thunderstorms off the higher terrain of southern Montana and northern Wyoming.  These storms eventually became severe as they moved northeastward through eastern Montana.

Strong pressure gradients along the Beartooth/Absaroka Foothills, along with strong northwest winds behind a cold front, resulted in high wind gusts across many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts ranged from 62-67 mph across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong pressure gradients along the Beartooth/Absaroka Foothills, along with strong northwest winds behind a cold front, resulted in high wind gusts across many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted 75+ mph in the Nye vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable atmosphere combined with warm moist air from the southeast and a cold front from the west, to easily form and support multiple severe thunderstorms which merged into a bowing squall line and resulted in a tornado and a gustnado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter 8 N Malta recorded an 88 mph wind gust on their personal home weather station.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a 61 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front from the west interacted with a warm moist air mass and an unstable atmosphere over eastern Montana to produce several strong thunderstorms, a few of which became severe over Dawson, Richland, and Wibaux Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site (MTBVR), a 58 mph wind gust was recorded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a funnel cloud near Plentywood, which was coincident with rotation seen on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure at the surface combined with a very unstable atmosphere and a hot moist air from the south to quickly form strong and severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours, which moved northward through the eastern edge of Montana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough brought up to 60 kt cross barrier flow and lee side surface low for strong winds along the Rocky Mountain Front. Unlike most of the November wind events in 2020, strong wind gusts did not move onto the plains of Central/North-central MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT Two Medicine Bridge station 58 kt (67 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cross barrier flow developed over the Rocky Mountain Front and Central/North-central plains as high pressure ridging aloft was flattened by subtle Pacific disturbances. Tightening of the lower level pressure gradient was observed as a strong surface high remained anchored southwest of the Divide. These factors combined with mountain wave activity brought the final high wind event for November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MDT Two Medicine Bridge station (1 ENE East Glacier) measured a 65 kt (75 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable southwesterly flow aloft developed over the region on Friday July 3rd and continued through Wednesday July 8th when a Pacific trough displaced moisture and instability to the east. There were mostly sub-severe hail and winds gusts reported for a majority of days during this evening but the aforementioned trough provided adequate wind shear (30 to 55 kts), CAPE (1500 to 3500 j/kg), and moisture (td 50s and 60s) for more widespread severe weather on Tuesday July 7th. Over two dozen reports of severe hail and wind gusts were reported as storms developed over the southwest and moved northeastward into Central and North-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station K7S6 3.6 S White Sulphur Springs reported a thunderstorm wind gust of 53 kts (61 mph).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable southwesterly flow aloft developed over the region on Friday July 3rd and continued through Wednesday July 8th when a Pacific trough displaced moisture and instability to the east. There were mostly sub-severe hail and winds gusts reported for a majority of days during this evening but the aforementioned trough provided adequate wind shear (30 to 55 kts), CAPE (1500 to 3500 j/kg), and moisture (td 50s and 60s) for more widespread severe weather on Tuesday July 7th. Over two dozen reports of severe hail and wind gusts were reported as storms developed over the southwest and moved northeastward into Central and North-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported downed 15 to 20 foot crab apple tree and flag pole due to thunderstorm wind gusts. Nearly an inch of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm development underway across SW and central MT lifts northeast across central and eastern portions of north-central MT through the evening. Warm/dry conditions in the lower levels set up a favorable environment for strong outflow winds with these storms along with some potential for hail in the strongest cells. Additional thunderstorm development likely later during the afternoon and evening hours over western MT ahead of the main upper level trough moving inland across WA. This activity brings another round showers and thunderstorms later in the evening. Surface cold front ahead of the trough moves eastward across the continental divide and sweeps eastward across most of the forecast area overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station ekhm8, Elkhorn raws - 8 W Townsend.
