EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwave trough moves into The Northern Rockies and continues east-northeastward across Montana. Thunderstorm development occurs across the mountains during the early afternoon hours, with coverage expected to increase as the shortwave moves eastward. Greatest instability and potential for organized storm updrafts with hail and locally heavy|rain, in addition to strong winds, exists early in the afternoon with initial storms. Then, transition to more of a widespread thunderstorm wind threat as storms track eastward into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail size reported to have ranged from pea-size to golf ball size, with winds of 26 mph, and torrential downpour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms develop over western Montana as daytime heating continues to destabilize the atmosphere. These showers and thunderstorms push into the area, and with additional development expected over the mountains in the southern half of the TFX CWA, a decent sized area of thunderstorms moves through the southeastern 2/3 to 3/4 of the CWA. SPC continues to maintain a marginal risk for severe weather over the southern|portion of our CWA, which makes sense given the very dry boundary layer and DCAPE values between 800-1000 J/kg in that area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Asos measured wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively strong Pacific trough and surface cold front moved through the region on September 24th, 2020, bring a round of strong westerly winds to Rocky Mountain Front and North-central plains. H700 winds were mostly in the 45 to 55 mph range, which did mix down along and behind the surface cold front. Much of Central/North-central MT was included in a High Wind Warning, though most of the wind reports were observed along the Rocky Mountain Front and Hi-Line areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 51 kt (59 mph) non thunderstorm wind gust was observed at 7 ENE Denton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough developed over the Northern Rockies and delivered a shot of unseasonably cool air along with rain and mountain snow. A southward advancing cold front, supported by a 1040 mb Canadian surface high, raced through Southwest Montana. This resulted in a tight pressure gradient and gusty northwest winds, particularly for the north to south orientated valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An AWOS station located at the Twin Bridges Airport measured a 51 kt (59 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With lower pressure toward the northeast and higher pressure toward the southwest, persistent surface winds from the west and northwest spread across the region. Atmospheric conditions were favorable to allow some higher gusts from aloft to reach the surface, especially through Sioux Pass in Richland County on the 1st. These similar conditions were even more favorable for stronger and more widespread high winds on the 2nd, when fire weather conditions were ripe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Huff fire began on September 2nd on private land and was quickly spread toward the southeast by sustained winds of 45 mph and wind gusts up to 61 mph, narrowly missing the town of Jordan and crossing over Highway 200.  The fire was not completely contained until the morning of September 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms move across northeastern portions of the CWA as a cold front pushes through the region. While southern areas of the CWA are quiet during the morning hours, daytime heating causes these areas to become unstable, with SPC mesoanalysis showing CAPE values of upwards of 1500J/kg. Showers and thunderstorms are expected to rapidly develop. With model soundings showing a classic inverted-V shape, DCAPE values are also fairly high, up to 1300J/kg, so any shower or thunderstorm will have the potential to produce gusty winds, and small hail may reach the ground at times thanks to the dry air at the surface. Showers and storms will likely continue into late evening, but should move eastward out of our area by mid to late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Via social media. Time and exact location estimated by radar and social media report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The BobCat fire was detected on September 2nd on private lands approximately 7 miles southeast of Roundup east of MT Highway 87. The fire burned in tall grass, intermixed with ponderosa pine stands and sagebrush. Primarily wind-driven, the fire spread to the southeast from the origin. The total number of personnel working the fire was 104. Total acreage burned was 30,030. The Musselshell County DES reported that a structure assessment found 14 primary and 34 secondary/outbuildings were lost in the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cold air rushed over the Divide early on the 7th resulting in strong, damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed trees were reported southeast of Eureka near Glen Lake, Marion, Dickey Lake, near Yaak, and also Troy. Power lines were damaged with isolated power outages near Eureka, more widespread power outages occurred around Troy and Yaak. A common theme from spotters was a few trees down on their property but not widespread tree damage. Though damage occurred, locals noted that this event was far less impactful than previous wind events last fall and winter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 2nd, A dry but vigorous shortwave and attendant cold front moved through the region via a dry northwesterly flow aloft. Ahead of the cold front, strong westerly winds combined with low humidity and dry fuels to create critical fire weather conditions over portions of North Central and Southwest Montana. Then the aforementioned cold front sharply shifted winds to the northwest, creating challenges for the fire community. There were at least four known new wildfire starts in the Great Falls CWA. One wildfire, named the Birdseye Fire, located on the north side of Helena prompted evacuations for local residents and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 4 pm MDT, the Birdseye Wildfire broke out on the north side of Helena and grew to about 613 acres. Red Flag conditions preceded the fire start, with westerly gusting near 40 mph and relative humidity near 15%. A slightly cooler and more humid air mass moved in around 5 pm MDT as winds shifted to the north and eventually diminished.  Evacuations were ordered for areas south of Lincoln Rd, west of Green Meadow and north of Custer but were lifted after 830 pm MDT according to local media. No injuries, fatalities, or property damage was reported with this fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cold air rushed over the Divide early on the 7th resulting in strong, damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging north to northeast winds began in the Lake Blaine area early in the morning then moved southward to Flathead Lake. A peak wind gust of 75 mph led to several houses losing shingles around Lake Blaine, a roof blown off a shed, and multiple power outages. Further south, downed trees and associated power outages were focused around Flathead Lake, especially along Finley Point. Several docks were damaged or destroyed on the south and west side of Flathead Lake by waves of up to 6 feet, and  one pontoon boat was torn apart. Power outages impacted several thousand customers.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts estimated at 50 to 55 mph caused numerous downed trees from Thompson Falls northwestward to the Idaho border along and adjacent to Highway 200. Power outages were common.  At least 2 vehicles were damaged, as was a fifth wheel trailer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak shortwave troughs interacted with lingering monsoon moisture and sufficient diurnal heating set the conditions for thunderstorm development over potions of Central and Southwest Montana on Wednesday and Thursday (Aug 26th, 27th). This was followed by a short period of much cooler wetter weather for the final weekend in August as a potent longwave trough dived southeast from the Gulf of Alaska. This short lived cool down ended when high pressure ridging returned to the region in early September and weak shortwaves provided periods of windy but warm and dry conditions. This sequence of meteorological occurrences created a set up for a lightning hold over fire in the foothills northeast of Bozeman to become the significant Bridger Foothills Wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning hold out fire was discovered in the foothills northeast of Bozeman when dry conditions began to feed its growth. the fire was initially only able to grow to about 400 acres but then exploded to 7000 acres the next day as red flag conditions moved into the region. At one point, the fire was classified as a type 1 incident and eventually grew to 8224 acres. The fire persisted through mid- October and destroyed 68 structures, including 30 residential homes. It caused damage to 11 structures, of which five were residences. The containment cost was $7.225 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the previous snow storm moved away from the region, a cold Canadian air mass took its place. As this colder air interacted with the warmer surface waters of Fort Peck Lake, a light south-easterly wind directed the resulting low clouds and fog northward and eastward, which spread a layer of dense fog along and north of US Highway 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Glasgow airport ASOS site (KGGW), visibility of a quarter mile or less persisted for almost 9 hours through the early morning of November 11th.

The peak wind gust of 60 mph was measured at 1:07 AM on November 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific cold front brought a wintry mix of precipitation across northwest Montana. Freezing rain was observed as valley cold air pools kept ground temperatures at or below freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A glaze of ice due to freezing rain led to the closure of I-90 from Lookout Pass to St. Regis. I-90 was closed for approximately three hours. Cold ground temperatures allowed for ice accumulation to occur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls developed along the arctic cold frontal boundary along portions of US Highway 2 and 93 across northwest Montana during the morning hours of December 22. Snow squalls also impacted US Highway 12 and I-90 during the evening hours of December 22 as the arctic front moved across the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow squall impacted US highway 12 from MacDonald Pass to Garrison and the I-90 corridor from Drummond through Deer Lodge. Department of highway officials reported a flash freeze of roadways as road temperatures dropped from the mid 30s to upper 20s. Visibility below one quarter of a mile and wind gusts in excess of 20 mph where observed. Highway officials reported 3 to 5 inches of snow in less than two hours from Garrison to Deer Lodge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls developed along the arctic cold frontal boundary along portions of US Highway 2 and 93 across northwest Montana during the morning hours of December 22. Snow squalls also impacted US Highway 12 and I-90 during the evening hours of December 22 as the arctic front moved across the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whiteout conditions were observed from Whitefish to Eureka on US Highway 93 as a snow squall moved southward. Roadways quickly became snow-covered and icy as temperatures dropped from the mid to upper 30s into the upper 20s to low 30s. Visibility less than one quarter mile and wind gusts in excess of 20 mph were observed. A trained spotter, located 4 miles northeast of Rexford, reported 2 inches of snow in a one hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 7th, deep troughing over the western US kept Montana in an area of large scale ascent and upper level divergence, allowing H700 mb and surface low pressure systems to deepen over Eastern Idaho and move northeastward through Eastern Montana and the Dakotas. This is a favorable track to bring cold temperatures, gusty winds, and sufficient moisture for accumulating snow to Central/North-central Montana. The snowfall was enhanced by northerly upslope flow over the plains onto the higher terrain in addition to the moisture wrapping around the northwest side of the low pressure system.  Snow and gusty northerly winds initially developed over the Northern Rocky Mountain Front early Saturday November 7th, before expanding south and eastward through the remainder of the region Saturday night into Sunday. At the height of the storm, winds gusted into the 40 to 60 mph range, resulting in blizzard conditions over much of Central and North-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds up to 43 mph with gusts up to 53 mph combined with falling snow for blizzard conditions at 2 SE Browning.

The Glacier Park International Airport temporarily closed and trained spotters in the Flathead Valley reported 2 to 3 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A North Pacific trough passed through the area quickly between November 30 and December 1. This resulted in some gusty winds on the 30th, as well as some snow, especially to the Little Belt Mountains, the Snowy Mountains, and areas in between. Snowfall tapered off early morning hours on the 1st as the trough exited eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports 6 of snow at 7SSE Neihart. Snow began around 2130 on 11/30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasonal weather pattern continued to steer most precipitation away from the northeast corner and the eastern edge of Montana through the month. For some locations, severe drought conditions retreated slightly eastward (Daniels and northern Valley Counties). For other locations, severe drought conditions expanded slightly southward (Richland County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions (D2-Severe) persisted across all of Sheridan County through the entire month of December.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions (D2-Severe) persisted over east-central and northeastern portions of Richland County through the month.  Over the remainder of the eastern edge of the county, generally near and east of MT Highway 16, drought conditions (D2-Severe) began in the second week of the month and lasted through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 7th, deep troughing over the western US kept Montana in an area of large scale ascent and upper level divergence, allowing H700 mb and surface low pressure systems to deepen over Eastern Idaho and move northeastward through Eastern Montana and the Dakotas. This is a favorable track to bring cold temperatures, gusty winds, and sufficient moisture for accumulating snow to Central/North-central Montana. The snowfall was enhanced by northerly upslope flow over the plains onto the higher terrain in addition to the moisture wrapping around the northwest side of the low pressure system.  Snow and gusty northerly winds initially developed over the Northern Rocky Mountain Front early Saturday November 7th, before expanding south and eastward through the remainder of the region Saturday night into Sunday. At the height of the storm, winds gusted into the 40 to 60 mph range, resulting in blizzard conditions over much of Central and North-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 200 was closed from Eddies Corner to Lewistown due to blizzard conditions. Message relayed via MDT twitter.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cut Bank Airport observed sustained winds above 35 mph with gusts up to 50 mph, which combined with falling snow for blizzard conditions. Severe driving conditions reported on State route 213 from Cut Bank to the Canadian border at 9 am lst. Message relayed by MDT twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A North Pacific trough passed through the area quickly between November 30 and December 1. This resulted in some gusty winds on the 30th, as well as some snow, especially to the Little Belt Mountains, the Snowy Mountains, and areas in between. Snowfall tapered off early morning hours on the 1st as the trough exited eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from social media of 4.5 of snow at Lewistown MDT office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening upper level ridge allowed a Pacific weather system to advance into the area. As this occurred, a surface pressure gradient developed along the Continental Divide, with a 23 mb difference between the high over Western Montana and the low over East-Central MT, and this pressure gradient would remain through the day. This prompted the issuance of High Wind Warnings along the Rocky Mountain Front. Mid and upper level winds would strengthen overnight Monday and into Tuesday as zonal flow became more became more established. This would result in a few scattered high wind gusts across the plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mdt station at east glacier two medicine bridge measured a 75 mph non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved from the Pacific Northwest to the Great Basin, and produced some upslope snow showers across North-Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports 3 to 4 inches of snow from wednesday night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasonal weather pattern continued to steer most precipitation away from the northeast corner and the eastern edge of Montana through the month. For some locations, severe drought conditions retreated slightly eastward (Daniels and northern Valley Counties). For other locations, severe drought conditions expanded slightly southward (Richland County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions (D2-Severe) persisted across central and eastern portions of Daniels County (generally near and east of MT Highway 13) through the entire month of December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The weather pattern continued to steer most precipitation away from the region, which allowed drought conditions (D2-Severe) to enter Wibaux County from the east beginning in the second week of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought Conditions (D2-Severe) over the northeastern half of Wibaux County began in the second week of December and persisted there through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of three weather systems moved through Montana between December 18 and 21, 2020, bringing strong winds with the first two systems on the 18-19th. Winds increased to 20-35 MPH with gusts to 40 MPH during the overnight hours on the 18-19. Winds would continue to increase a little through the morning, but weakening a bit in the afternoon.||Winds would pick up later in the evening on the evening of the 19th as the second system began to push into the area. This system would be the windiest of the three, with winds aloft at 700 mb increasing to 70kts/80MPH. These strong winds would mix down to the surface, resulting in wind gusts in excess of 70 and even 80 MPH. High Wind Warnings were issued across the area, especially across the North-Central Plains. ||The third and final system would move into the area on Tuesday, which would go on to result in some mountain snow and gusty winds across the plains, but this will be recorded in a separate episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station fblm8, fort belknap raws.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow aloft began to increase ahead of a broad trough over the Pacific Northwest. H700 winds of 60 to 80 kts were common and a stable layer near the mountain top level created favorable conditions for breaking mountain waves. At the surface, a couple of lee side troughs moved through the Canadian prairies, which provided a tight pressure gradient over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 48 kt wind gust measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For this time of year, after a warmer day on the 9th, recent additional melting of the patchy snow pack and some wet ground around the region provided some additional moisture near the surface.  Early on the morning on the 10th, as temperatures dropped well into the teens, conditions were favorable for some areas of freezing fog development, which persisted off and on for a few days in this stagnant weather pattern.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Glasgow airport ASOS site (KGGW), visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less for 7 hours through the day on the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a warm front that pushed through the area on the the 21st, weak easterly flow along with clearing skies resulted in a widespread dense fog developing across the plains of North-Central Montana. This dense fog was mainly an issue during the overnight hours, as strong winds moving in would result in the mixing out of this fog bank.||During the day on Tuesday (the 22nd), a well defined shortwave trough moved through the area, with an attendant surface low pressure system that emerged east of the Rockies in SW Alberta, deepening as it did so. This system would bring a Pacific cold front through the area early in the morning. Behind the cold front, winds would increase and support gusts to 35-45 mph across most of the plains. There was a risk of stronger winds further to the east, and a High Wind Warning was issued for Blaine, Fergus, and Judith Basin counties due to this threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Choteau airport shows period of dense fog with visibilities under 1/2mi at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of three weather systems moved through Montana between December 18 and 21, 2020, bringing strong winds with the first two systems on the 18-19th. Winds increased to 20-35 MPH with gusts to 40 MPH during the overnight hours on the 18-19. Winds would continue to increase a little through the morning, but weakening a bit in the afternoon.||Winds would pick up later in the evening on the evening of the 19th as the second system began to push into the area. This system would be the windiest of the three, with winds aloft at 700 mb increasing to 70kts/80MPH. These strong winds would mix down to the surface, resulting in wind gusts in excess of 70 and even 80 MPH. High Wind Warnings were issued across the area, especially across the North-Central Plains. ||The third and final system would move into the area on Tuesday, which would go on to result in some mountain snow and gusty winds across the plains, but this will be recorded in a separate episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station bbslh, 10.2 ne bozeman (avalanche).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a warm front that pushed through the area on the the 21st, weak easterly flow along with clearing skies resulted in a widespread dense fog developing across the plains of North-Central Montana. This dense fog was mainly an issue during the overnight hours, as strong winds moving in would result in the mixing out of this fog bank.||During the day on Tuesday (the 22nd), a well defined shortwave trough moved through the area, with an attendant surface low pressure system that emerged east of the Rockies in SW Alberta, deepening as it did so. This system would bring a Pacific cold front through the area early in the morning. Behind the cold front, winds would increase and support gusts to 35-45 mph across most of the plains. There was a risk of stronger winds further to the east, and a High Wind Warning was issued for Blaine, Fergus, and Judith Basin counties due to this threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Great Falls airport reports visibility 1/2 mi or less in fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast jet stream aloft and a tight pressure gradient over the region, set up a favorable atmosphere for mixing most of those stronger winds down to the surface for many locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the town of Zortman and the immediate surrounding area in southwest Phillips County, wind gusts of 61 mph were measured for a few hours in the morning of the 19th. More widespread and longer-duration severe winds occurred on the 20th, especially downwind of the Little Rocky Mountains.  Sustained winds there of at least 40 mph began mid-morning and continued through mid-afternoon.  Severe wind gusts of at least 58 mph were common for 2 1/2 hours in the morning, then again for half an hour in the afternoon.  The peak wind gust of 69 mph was recorded at the Malta South US-191 DOT site (MTMAL), at 9:41 MST on the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast jet stream aloft and a tight pressure gradient over the region, set up a favorable atmosphere for mixing most of those stronger winds down to the surface for many locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Winnett mesonet site (GGWWT), peak wind gusts of 58 mph were recorded for a few hours during the afternoon of the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast jet stream aloft and a tight pressure gradient over the region, set up a favorable atmosphere for mixing most of those stronger winds down to the surface for many locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across western and central portions of Garfield County, severe wind gusts of at least 58 mph were common from late morning through early afternoon on the 20th. The peak wind gust of 67 mph was recorded at the Jordan airport ASOS site (KJDN) at 13:48 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast jet stream aloft and a tight pressure gradient over the region, set up a favorable atmosphere for mixing most of those stronger winds down to the surface for many locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe wind gusts of at least 58 mph were measured during the afternoon on both the 19th and the 20th at the King Coulee RAWS site (KIGM8).  The peak wind gust of 68 mph was recorded at 14:44 MST on the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast jet stream aloft and a tight pressure gradient over the region, set up a favorable atmosphere for mixing most of those stronger winds down to the surface for many locations across northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Glendive BLM mesonet site (MTM28), located 5 ESE Hoyt, severe wind gusts of at least 58 mph were measured during the afternoon both on the 19th and 20th.  The peak wind gust of 64 mph was recorded at 14:00 MST on the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly flow aloft began to increase ahead of a broad trough over the Pacific Northwest. H700 winds of 60 to 80 kts were common and a stable layer near the mountain top level created favorable conditions for breaking mountain waves. At the surface, a couple of lee side troughs moved through the Canadian prairies, which provided a tight pressure gradient over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cut Bank Airport measured a 53 kt (61 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On November 7th, deep troughing over the western US kept Montana in an area of large scale ascent and upper level divergence, allowing H700 mb and surface low pressure systems to deepen over Eastern Idaho and move northeastward through Eastern Montana and the Dakotas. This is a favorable track to bring cold temperatures, gusty winds, and sufficient moisture for accumulating snow to Central/North-central Montana. The snowfall was enhanced by northerly upslope flow over the plains onto the higher terrain in addition to the moisture wrapping around the northwest side of the low pressure system.  Snow and gusty northerly winds initially developed over the Northern Rocky Mountain Front early Saturday November 7th, before expanding south and eastward through the remainder of the region Saturday night into Sunday. At the height of the storm, winds gusted into the 40 to 60 mph range, resulting in blizzard conditions over much of Central and North-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow with winds gusting up to 37 mph brought blizzard conditions to the Lewistown Airport.

Snow and gusty northerly winds initially developed over the Northern Rocky Mountain Front early Saturday November 7th, before expanding south and eastward through the remainder of the region Saturday night into Sunday. At the height of the storm, winds gusted into the 40 to 60 mph range, resulting in blizzard conditions over much of Central and North-central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and winds gusting up to 50 mph combined for blizzard conditions at Inverness. Severe driving conditions were reported on highway 2 form the Liberty County line to Hingham at 720 am lst via twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of three weather systems moved through Montana between December 18 and 21, 2020, bringing strong winds with the first two systems on the 18-19th. Winds increased to 20-35 MPH with gusts to 40 MPH during the overnight hours on the 18-19. Winds would continue to increase a little through the morning, but weakening a bit in the afternoon.||Winds would pick up later in the evening on the evening of the 19th as the second system began to push into the area. This system would be the windiest of the three, with winds aloft at 700 mb increasing to 70kts/80MPH. These strong winds would mix down to the surface, resulting in wind gusts in excess of 70 and even 80 MPH. High Wind Warnings were issued across the area, especially across the North-Central Plains. ||The third and final system would move into the area on Tuesday, which would go on to result in some mountain snow and gusty winds across the plains, but this will be recorded in a separate episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Upper Elk Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture ahead of an upstream trough caused increasing cloudiness across the region from west to east. Perturbations in the flow of the overhead ridge caused mountain snow showers along the divide that would eventually expand to the mountains of Southwest and Central Montana. Snowfall accumulations would be light, with reports of 2-4 of snowfall across the passes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours on December 10th, satellite imagery and surface observations showed that low clouds and fog began to develop across Central and Northern Montana. This prompted a Dense Fog Advisory for all zones across Central and North Central Montana through the morning. Below freezing temperatures would result in icy areas, and at least one accident was reported along with the fog and subsequent ice accretion due to the fog and drizzle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicle accident near belt creek on montana highway 228. dense fog and freezing drizzle was observed prior to accident.

In addition, strong, gusty, northerly winds across Wheatland County produced blizzard conditions for a period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions were reported. The highway was closed from Harlowton to Eddies Corner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, October 23, Montana was in the crosshairs for a strong early winter snow storm set to begin later that afternoon afternoon and continue through Saturday evening. A strong upper level disturbance positioned over southeast Alaska would continue to move south-southeastward during the day, reaching the Pacific Northwest/British Columbia border by late in the afternoon. This feature would then approach western Montana overnight in conjunction with a strong surface cold front that would sweep southward out of Alberta into north central portions of the state. Widespread snow (heavy at times), blowing snow, and very cold wind chills were anticipated overnight and through the day on Saturday. Snow gradually tapered off Saturday night into early Sunday morning as the core of the upper level trough continued moving southward. Models were in excellent agreement on the details of this storm with very good run-to-run consistency so confidence was very high that impacts will be extensive and widespread. It appeared that the east slopes of the Rocky Mountains and much of central Montana would see upwards of 8 to 12 inches of snow accumulation, and some models even suggested that up to 18 inches of snow was not out of the question. The Winter Storm Warning remained in effect for this region, and was later upgraded to a Blizzard Warning for areas along the Rocky Mountain Front. Lesser amounts of snow were expected along eastern portions of the Hi-Line and in southwest Montana, so these areas were under a Winter Weather Advisory. Finally, a surface high pressure system would be centered over central and western Montana Sunday morning. This resulted in low temperatures on Sunday falling to near or below zero with many record temperatures expected to be set across the entire forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Co-op observer reports 6.5 of snow in Choteau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow aloft delivered gusty westerly surface winds, along with scattered showers and mountain snow along the Divide and over portions of Central/North-central Montana on Monday October 12th. In addition, a few of these showers produced some lightning strikes along with moderate downpours of rain. This activity was expected to continue into the evening hours, before diminishing with the loss of daytime heating. The High Wind warning was continued due to sustained winds and gusts remained within striking distance of criteria values. There was a brief period of quiet weather as |transient ridging quickly traversed the region Monday night. The first sign of|the next weather maker was felt along the Divide Tuesday morning as a brief period of accumulating snow developed for locations above 5000 ft. The Winter Weather Advisory still looked good as of Monday afternoon as it appeared there could be some impacts to travel and outdoor interests before the warmer air moves in and inhibited snow accumulation. This area of precipitation then moved over the remainder of the CWA by Tuesday afternoon but snow levels were expected to rise above 7000 ft by afternoon. ||The focus then began to shift toward the potential strong winds from Tuesday afternoon into Wednesday morning as a wide swath of very strong upper level winds move into North-central and Southwest Montana. There was still some uncertainty on Monday whether these winds would translate to the surface wind gusts in excess of 60 mph, but the possibility looked best over the higher terrain. The area of greatest concern was areas south and west of a Lewistown, to Great Falls, to Cut Bank line where the strongest H700 winds of 50-70kts were expected to reside through Wednesday morning; which had the potential to make travel over mountain passes like MacDonald, Bozeman, Norris Hill, and Deep Creek hazardous as this looked like an ideal setup for high winds to materialize given a strong west- to-east flow. Given the situation, the current High Wind Watch was continued. Late Tuesday night into Wednesday morning, snow levels began to fall once again as colder air filled in on the back side of the system. This is when some lower elevation locations had the potential to see some light |accumulating snow over portions of North-central Montana, in addition to locations along the divide and the Central Mountains. Snowfall accumulations of 3-6 appeared possible along the Continental Divide and in the mountains of Central and North |Central Montana during the day on Wednesday, with even a dusting to up upwards of 2 over portions of the plains. especially along the Montana Hwy 200 corridor. A Winter Weather Advisory was issued on Tuesday for this snow. In addition to precipitation, another bout of high wind criteria wind speeds/gusts occurred during the morning and early afternoon hours across nearly the entire CWA (save for the Hi-Line east of Shelby) on Wednesday. Some mountain snow did occur Wednesday night that was reported early Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station wrsm8 wise river raws.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With respect to wind, south to north orientated H700 jet max of 35-50kts/H500 jet max of 50-75kts remains over southwest and into central Montana through 03-06z Sunday before sliding eastward over The Northern High Plains. Deep diurnal mixing combined with terrain funneling, most notably in the north-south orientated valleys, continues to lead to sustained winds of 35-45 mph and gusts approaching 65 mph across southwest and into portions of central Montana. High Wind Warning continues for Beaverhead, |Madison, Gallatin, Jefferson, and Broadwater counties. While a few isolated wind gusts above High Wind criteria occur north of the High Wind Warning in Central Montana (Porphyry Peak in the Little Belt Mountains and Judith Peak in the Judith Mountains), have held off on expanding the High Wind Warning further north given the isolated nature of the gusts. With that being said, concern is that the H700/500 jet max slides further north between 21z Saturday and 03z Sunday that locations like Great Falls, Belt, and Stanford could briefly reach High Wind criteria. ||With respect to the potential for severe weather, the Storm Prediction Center continues to maintain a slight risk for portions of northwest Montana, mainly northwest of a line from Augusta, to Choteau, to Conrad, to Cut Bank. Afternoon storms are on-going across western Montana, with additional development expected along The Continental Divide through 00z Sunday. Hi-Res models also indicate the potential for storms to develop over far eastern portions of central and north central Montana before lifting north/northeast into southern Canada, but confidence in coverage and intensity of these storms is not as high as across northwest Montana. Large hail up to the size |of half dollar and damaging winds up to 60 mph are the primary threats with any severe thunderstorm, especially across the Slight Risk area. In addition to the threat for |severe weather, the potential for locally heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding also exists, especially where storms train over complex terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Asos station KCTB Cut Bank Airport. Likely outflow from a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong on cold arctic air mass descending southward from Canada directed plentiful Pacific moisture up and over the northern Rocky Mountains. Accumulating snowfall was common through central and southern Montana with lesser amounts toward the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter at the Buckhorn General Store in Zortman estimated 7 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold arctic air mass directed a slow-moving cold front and ample Pacific moisture across the region from northern through eastern Montana.  During the evening of the 21st, some areas received heavy snow with snowfall rate exceeding 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple snowfall reports from trained spotters across eastern portions of northern Phillips County ranged from 6 to 10 inches.  The highest amount of 10 inches was reported at 10 NE Whitewater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening surface low positioned over southern Alberta on October 16th generated strong southwest winds winds across the region during the day on Friday. The strongest winds were along the Rocky Mountain Front. As of 3 PM, short-term model guidance was supporting the expectation that winds would spread onto the adjacent plains and across North-central Montana late in the afternoon into the evening, once the surface low began to pull away from the Rocky Mountain Front. Thus, high wind warnings remained in effect at least through early evening.||Areas of light rain and mountain snow were ongoing Friday afternoon. These were associated with weak warm advection aloft, mainly for areas mainly north and east of|a line from Great Falls to Cut Bank. This activity appeared likely to continue through the evening, along with continuing snow over the Northern Rockies. ||As the surface low moved away to the east, the cold front would be drawn southward into North-central Montana. This was expected to occur by around 9pm for the HiLine, midnight for central MT, and early Saturday morning for south-central Montana. As the front moved by, light rain developed, which then changed over to snow. Initially wet roads were expected to freeze by morning, especially the bridges and overpasses. Wet snow sticking and freezing to power lines and branches had the potential to cause some isolated power outages. Rain and snowfall amounts were expected to be light across the North-central plains as the shallow front slid by. Some orographic enhancement in the northwest flow aloft had the potential to help maintain precipitation into Saturday for upslope areas along the Rocky Mountain Front and the upslope areas of central Montana. The cold air behind the front was shallow, and given northwesterly flow aloft, model guidance suggested the front would stall out somewhere north of I-90. Although this was not explicitly forecast at the time time, we would need to monitor for the possibility of the development of snow bands along this frontal convergence zone early|Saturday morning. Total snowfall amounts from this first wave had trended lower in Friday's updated forecast guidance. However, the forecaster noted that regardless of snow totals, any icing that developed with this early season event would be worth noting and extra precautions should be taken while driving.||The first set of Winter Weather Advisories remained in effect until 6 pm Saturday for north-central areas and midnight Saturday night for the southwest. A second disturbance would allow very little time to pass before the next round of snow and perhaps some mixed precipitation moved in late Saturday night and into Sunday. This system was a bit warmer but it it was a tricky forecast to know how much cold air would remain in the lower levels. There was potential that snow may become mixed with rain or even freezing drizzle at times, which will ultimately determine how much snow accumulation is realized. Most of this precipitation was expected to fall Sunday morning and afternoon but there was potential for some to linger through Monday morning. This resulted in another set of Winter Weather Advisories being issued from midnight Saturday through noon on Monday, for roughly the same areas as the first set that had been issued. Confidence was lowest in the Southwest due to the fact that warmer air may infiltrate before the most significant precipitation fell, but the forecaster decided to keep them in it since a little shift in timing could produce more impactful results. As mentioned earlier, forecast confidence in snowfall totals remain low but areas along the Divide and the central mountains had the potential to receive snow totals up to 6 inches or more in some cases. High temperatures for this afternoon were to be the coldest of the season thus far, with highs struggling to reach the 30s over much of Central/North-central Montana, with warmer 30s and 40s over the southwest. The cold temperatures would continue on Sunday but were expected to be slightly warmer than Saturday. More seasonal temperatures were expected by Monday afternoon after a brief period of patchy freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured 13.3 inches between saturday and sunday, with 9 inches new since saturday night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, October 23, Montana was in the crosshairs for a strong early winter snow storm set to begin later that afternoon afternoon and continue through Saturday evening. A strong upper level disturbance positioned over southeast Alaska would continue to move south-southeastward during the day, reaching the Pacific Northwest/British Columbia border by late in the afternoon. This feature would then approach western Montana overnight in conjunction with a strong surface cold front that would sweep southward out of Alberta into north central portions of the state. Widespread snow (heavy at times), blowing snow, and very cold wind chills were anticipated overnight and through the day on Saturday. Snow gradually tapered off Saturday night into early Sunday morning as the core of the upper level trough continued moving southward. Models were in excellent agreement on the details of this storm with very good run-to-run consistency so confidence was very high that impacts will be extensive and widespread. It appeared that the east slopes of the Rocky Mountains and much of central Montana would see upwards of 8 to 12 inches of snow accumulation, and some models even suggested that up to 18 inches of snow was not out of the question. The Winter Storm Warning remained in effect for this region, and was later upgraded to a Blizzard Warning for areas along the Rocky Mountain Front. Lesser amounts of snow were expected along eastern portions of the Hi-Line and in southwest Montana, so these areas were under a Winter Weather Advisory. Finally, a surface high pressure system would be centered over central and western Montana Sunday morning. This resulted in low temperatures on Sunday falling to near or below zero with many record temperatures expected to be set across the entire forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Co-op observer reports 6.5 of snow in Choteau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow aloft delivered gusty westerly surface winds, along with scattered showers and mountain snow along the Divide and over portions of Central/North-central Montana on Monday October 12th. In addition, a few of these showers produced some lightning strikes along with moderate downpours of rain. This activity was expected to continue into the evening hours, before diminishing with the loss of daytime heating. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge with an axis extending from the interior western US north into BC would be flattened some on Sunday by a series of very weak mid-level shortwave disturbances moving in from off the WA coast. Flow aloft turns to the west tonight, which would allow some very dry air to shift east into the region by Sunday morning. Surface pressure pattern also supported a shift to westerly winds by Sunday morning as low pressure developed east of the Rockies near the MT/AB border. An increase in mid level westerly winds and supporting surface pressure gradient would bring breezy to windy conditions for much of the area on Sunday with deeper mixing in the afternoon tapping into an area of 30-40kt mid-level winds over portions of central/N-central MT. |Afternoon temperatures would reach the upper 70s to low 80s for most lower elevation areas, with humidity falling below 20% across much of central MT. The combination of unseasonably warm and dry conditions along with gusty winds would bring increased fire danger, particularly in grassland and lower elevation areas across central MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire was reported on the Judith Musselshell Ranger District on the afternoon of October 3,2020.  The Yogo Fire is estimated to be over 500 acres in size and is located in the Little Belt Mountains around 1.5 miles south of Yogo Peak.  As of 6:50 p.m. on Saturday, October 3, the fire has made it to Prospect Ridge and is producing spot fires on the east side of the Ridge. Judith Basin County is working on issuing pre-evacuation notices to residences in the Middle Fork of the Judith River. ||���We are urging all hunters and hikers to avoid the area,��� said Judith Musselshell District Ranger Ron B. Wiseman.  ���Please continue to follow the Judith County Sheriff���s Office for the latest evacuation information and anticipate road and trail closures to be coming soon.���||The Incident Commander on the fire is using aviation resources, including a type 2 helicopter and an air tanker, to check the fire spread to the west and south.  Air Attack, two helicopters, and district resources are working to contain the fire and a Type III Incident Commander will be on scene this evening to manage all committed resources. The cause of the fire is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening surface low positioned over southern Alberta on October 16th generated strong southwest winds winds across the region during the day on Friday. The strongest winds were along the Rocky Mountain Front. As of 3 PM, short-term model guidance was supporting the expectation that winds would spread onto the adjacent plains and across North-central Montana late in the afternoon into the evening, once the surface low began to pull away from the Rocky Mountain Front. Thus, high wind warnings remained in effect at least through early evening.||Areas of light rain and mountain snow were ongoing Friday afternoon. These were associated with weak warm advection aloft, mainly for areas mainly north and east of|a line from Great Falls to Cut Bank. This activity appeared likely to continue through the evening, along with continuing snow over the Northern Rockies. ||As the surface low moved away to the east, the cold front would be drawn southward into North-central Montana. This was expected to occur by around 9pm for the HiLine, midnight for central MT, and early Saturday morning for south-central Montana. As the front moved by, light rain developed, which then changed over to snow. Initially wet roads were expected to freeze by morning, especially the bridges and overpasses. Wet snow sticking and freezing to power lines and branches had the potential to cause some isolated power outages. Rain and snowfall amounts were expected to be light across the North-central plains as the shallow front slid by. Some orographic enhancement in the northwest flow aloft had the potential to help maintain precipitation into Saturday for upslope areas along the Rocky Mountain Front and the upslope areas of central Montana. The cold air behind the front was shallow, and given northwesterly flow aloft, model guidance suggested the front would stall out somewhere north of I-90. Although this was not explicitly forecast at the time time, we would need to monitor for the possibility of the development of snow bands along this frontal convergence zone early|Saturday morning. Total snowfall amounts from this first wave had trended lower in Friday's updated forecast guidance. However, the forecaster noted that regardless of snow totals, any icing that developed with this early season event would be worth noting and extra precautions should be taken while driving.||The first set of Winter Weather Advisories remained in effect until 6 pm Saturday for north-central areas and midnight Saturday night for the southwest. A second disturbance would allow very little time to pass before the next round of snow and perhaps some mixed precipitation moved in late Saturday night and into Sunday. This system was a bit warmer but it it was a tricky forecast to know how much cold air would remain in the lower levels. There was potential that snow may become mixed with rain or even freezing drizzle at times, which will ultimately determine how much snow accumulation is realized. Most of this precipitation was expected to fall Sunday morning and afternoon but there was potential for some to linger through Monday morning. This resulted in another set of Winter Weather Advisories being issued from midnight Saturday through noon on Monday, for roughly the same areas as the first set that had been issued. Confidence was lowest in the Southwest due to the fact that warmer air may infiltrate before the most significant precipitation fell, but the forecaster decided to keep them in it since a little shift in timing could produce more impactful results. As mentioned earlier, forecast confidence in snowfall totals remain low but areas along the Divide and the central mountains had the potential to receive snow totals up to 6 inches or more in some cases. High temperatures for this afternoon were to be the coldest of the season thus far, with highs struggling to reach the 30s over much of Central/North-central Montana, with warmer 30s and 40s over the southwest. The cold temperatures would continue on Sunday but were expected to be slightly warmer than Saturday. More seasonal temperatures were expected by Monday afternoon after a brief period of patchy freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report of 8 of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold arctic air mass directed a slow-moving cold front and ample Pacific moisture across the region from northern through eastern Montana.  During the evening of the 21st, some areas received heavy snow with snowfall rate exceeding 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along and just north of US Highway 2 through central Phillips County, snowfall amounts ranging from 5 to 8 inches were reported.  The greatest measurement of 8 inches came from an NWS employee located 2N Saco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light winds combined with a cold and very moist boundary layer early on the 21sth, which had allowed areas of dense fog to develop over an area that roughly extended from Great Falls to Cut Bank. The Dense Fog Advisories would be cancelled or expire from north to south as the morning wore on as a cold front approached and low to middle level clouds worked against fog maintenance. Once the fog dissipated, the focus then shifted toward a vigorous Canadian cold front that crossed the international border |early in the morning before traversing the remainder of the CWA through the day. Snowfall amounts were generally expected to be in the 3 to 6 inch range for most areas across the plains, with slightly higher amounts in mountain areas and along the |Continental Divide. The Southwest valleys were expected to see the lowest |amounts, with total snowfall expected to be in the trace-3 range. Blowing snow due|to gusty northerly winds was also a concern, especially during the afternoon and evening for areas along and west of I-15 from Helena north to the Canadian border. No changes were made to the existing set of Winter Advisories with the morning forecast. Although areas along the Front had the potential to approach Winter Storm levels at times, it was initially decided to hold off on an upgrade due to a trend for lower snow amounts from model guidance, but the upgrade to a winter storm warning would be made later in the day.| |Behind the cold front, temperatures across North-Central Montana were expected to hold in the teens and 20s, but winds and snowfall would make it feel much colder. Temperatures were expected to fall into the single digits to teens across the entire CWA, and there was a possibility for some cold records to be set. Any lingering snow would diminish Thursday morning, but temperatures would struggle to to climb out of the teens across the north and 20s in the southwest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Co-op observer station cnkm8, chinook coop reported 6 of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, October 23, Montana was in the crosshairs for a strong early winter snow storm set to begin later that afternoon afternoon and continue through Saturday evening. A strong upper level disturbance positioned over southeast Alaska would continue to move south-southeastward during the day, reaching the Pacific Northwest/British Columbia border by late in the afternoon. This feature would then approach western Montana overnight in conjunction with a strong surface cold front that would sweep southward out of Alberta into north central portions of the state. Widespread snow (heavy at times), blowing snow, and very cold wind chills were anticipated overnight and through the day on Saturday. Snow gradually tapered off Saturday night into early Sunday morning as the core of the upper level trough continued moving southward. Models were in excellent agreement on the details of this storm with very good run-to-run consistency so confidence was very high that impacts will be extensive and widespread. It appeared that the east slopes of the Rocky Mountains and much of central Montana would see upwards of 8 to 12 inches of snow accumulation, and some models even suggested that up to 18 inches of snow was not out of the question. The Winter Storm Warning remained in effect for this region, and was later upgraded to a Blizzard Warning for areas along the Rocky Mountain Front. Lesser amounts of snow were expected along eastern portions of the Hi-Line and in southwest Montana, so these areas were under a Winter Weather Advisory. Finally, a surface high pressure system would be centered over central and western Montana Sunday morning. This resulted in low temperatures on Sunday falling to near or below zero with many record temperatures expected to be set across the entire forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station hrkm8 harkness raws.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another early season arctic airmass moved into the Billings Forecast Area toward the end of the month. At the same time, a strong upper level disturbance combined with moisture off the Pacific resulted in an overrunning event across south central Montana. The combination of these factors brought another round of heavy, wet snow to a large portion of the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall around 12 inches was reported across the area. Chains were required for the higher hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of central MT has already seen 1 to 2 of precipitation with locally higher amounts in central/northern Lewis and Clark County. An additional 0.5 to 1 of rainfall likely for the western portions of central Montana. Expect some flooding impacts to develop in the area highlighted by the Flood Watch along smaller creeks and streams and adjacent roadways as well as in lower lying areas where water collects. The Dearborn River is likely to see the sharpest increase, and will be closely monitored with latest forecasts projecting a rise to flood stage near Craig. The Sun, Teton and Marias Rivers will likely also rise, though not as sharply as the Dearborn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, October 23, Montana was in the crosshairs for a strong early winter snow storm set to begin later that afternoon afternoon and continue through Saturday evening. A strong upper level disturbance positioned over southeast Alaska would continue to move south-southeastward during the day, reaching the Pacific Northwest/British Columbia border by late in the afternoon. This feature would then approach western Montana overnight in conjunction with a strong surface cold front that would sweep southward out of Alberta into north central portions of the state. Widespread snow (heavy at times), blowing snow, and very cold wind chills were anticipated overnight and through the day on Saturday. Snow gradually tapered off Saturday night into early Sunday morning as the core of the upper level trough continued moving southward. Models were in excellent agreement on the details of this storm with very good run-to-run consistency so confidence was very high that impacts will be extensive and widespread. It appeared that the east slopes of the Rocky Mountains and much of central Montana would see upwards of 8 to 12 inches of snow accumulation, and some models even suggested that up to 18 inches of snow was not out of the question. The Winter Storm Warning remained in effect for this region, and was later upgraded to a Blizzard Warning for areas along the Rocky Mountain Front. Lesser amounts of snow were expected along eastern portions of the Hi-Line and in southwest Montana, so these areas were under a Winter Weather Advisory. Finally, a surface high pressure system would be centered over central and western Montana Sunday morning. This resulted in low temperatures on Sunday falling to near or below zero with many record temperatures expected to be set across the entire forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports 18 of heavy snow near Montana City via Twitter.

An additional 0.5 to 1 of rainfall likely for the western portions of central Montana. Expect some flooding impacts to develop in the area highlighted by the Flood Watch along smaller creeks and streams and adjacent roadways as well as in lower lying areas where water collects. The Dearborn River is likely to see the sharpest increase, and will be closely monitored with latest forecasts projecting a rise to flood stage near Craig. The Sun, Teton and Marias Rivers will likely also rise, though not as sharply as the Dearborn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front shifted east across the region during the afternoon of October 30, bringing gusty winds along and behind it. Wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were most widespread across north-central MT through the late afternoon. Gusts to 40 mph were likely across much of SW MT as well going into the evening with the strongest gusts through mountain passes.||While winds began to decrease during the evening, strong wind gusts lingered into the overnight period across eastern portions of north-central MT before winds aloft and the surface pressure gradient there relaxed. Favorable conditions for mountain wave wind enhancement were present along the immediate east slopes of the Rockies early in the evening, where some localized gusts to around 80 mph occurred, particularly west of Choteau and Bynum.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station brrm8 brenner raws.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, October 23, Montana was in the crosshairs for a strong early winter snow storm set to begin later that afternoon afternoon and continue through Saturday evening. A strong upper level disturbance positioned over southeast Alaska would continue to move south-southeastward during the day, reaching the Pacific Northwest/British Columbia border by late in the afternoon. This feature would then approach western Montana overnight in conjunction with a strong surface cold front that would sweep southward out of Alberta into north central portions of the state. Widespread snow (heavy at times), blowing snow, and very cold wind chills were anticipated overnight and through the day on Saturday. Snow gradually tapered off Saturday night into early Sunday morning as the core of the upper level trough continued moving southward. Models were in excellent agreement on the details of this storm with very good run-to-run consistency so confidence was very high that impacts will be extensive and widespread. It appeared that the east slopes of the Rocky Mountains and much of central Montana would see upwards of 8 to 12 inches of snow accumulation, and some models even suggested that up to 18 inches of snow was not out of the question. The Winter Storm Warning remained in effect for this region, and was later upgraded to a Blizzard Warning for areas along the Rocky Mountain Front. Lesser amounts of snow were expected along eastern portions of the Hi-Line and in southwest Montana, so these areas were under a Winter Weather Advisory. Finally, a surface high pressure system would be centered over central and western Montana Sunday morning. This resulted in low temperatures on Sunday falling to near or below zero with many record temperatures expected to be set across the entire forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports 12 of snow. Storm total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold, arctic air in place and the moisture from a Pacific low pressure system that tracked south and east of western Montana, created the perfect setup for heavy snow across western Montana. Widespread snowfall totals of near a foot were reported. The Potomac COOP reported a snowfall total of 18 inches on the 23rd, which is the highest, all-time 24 hour record since records began in 1964. Multiple stations reported their all-time coldest temperatures for the month of October few days later: 29 below zero in Potomac, 10 below at Seeley Lake and 7 below zero at Lindbergh Lake and Missoula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station located 32 miles south of Libby recorded 13 inches of snow which would be the all-time one day record for the month of October. Other snow amounts include: 10 inches 2 miles southwest of Marion, 8 inches in Libby and 2 inches in Eureka. A person on Twitter remarked that Highway 2 became a sheet of ice between Libby and Kalispell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The recently persistent multi-seasonal weather pattern has kept most storm systems away from the northeast corner of Montana. Severe drought (D-2) conditions crept into this area from the east, with the official Drought Monitor update released on October 1st. The area in severe drought (D-2) conditions persisted and expanded throughout the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions (D2- Severe) entered Eastern Roosevelt County from North Dakota to start the month of October. For the first half of October, it persisted mainly north of the US Highway 2 corridor; then by the 3rd week of the month, it overtook all of Eastern Roosevelt County, and persisted through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper disturbance moves eastward across the region, with a weak surface cold front moving eastward along and just ahead of the upper disturbance. Additionally, a northwest-southeast trough over north-central Montana serves as an additional focus of lift. Both surface boundaries result in low-level moisture convergence, but mainly focusing the best surface moisture across the north-central, central, and northeastern parts of the CWA. As a result, while showers possible just about anywhere in the CWA, the best chance for thunderstorms is mainly east of a line from Cut Bank to Fort Benton to Kings Hill Pass. With surface-based CAPE forecast over 1000 J/kg in most models, and mixed layer CAPE likely approaching if not exceeding 1000 J/kg, especially over Hill, Blaine, and NE'rn Chouteau, vigorous convection is possible. However, shear is minimal, with NW-W'ly winds of 10-25kts through the whole column, so storms are forecast to be very short-lived and/or 'pulse-y'. Some half-inch hail and gusty winds certainly possible. Severe hail or wind is not forecast. Showers and thunderstorms linger across north-central Montana through midnight, then die off overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half to one inch diameter hail reported. Minor street flooding and 53 mph wind gust also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms develop across western and southwest Montana, and continue to become more widespread. Daytime heating provides moderate instability for convective initiation. Inverted-v type soundings, characterized by a deep and dry boundary layer, support DCAPE values near 1000 J/kg. Thus, damaging wind gusts are the primary hazard associated with stronger thunderstorms. Shear profiles strengthen and may be enough to support a few supercell thunderstorms capable of producing locally heavy |rain, small hail, and with marginal support for an isolated larger hail event to the size of quarters. The severe storm potential peaks by early evening, although showers and thunderstorms continue to spread across the region overnight. This activity is associated with a passing upper level shortwave trough and its associated surface front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Corrected time. Reports strong winds brought rain into attic of house and knocked down trees. Also, reports a pile of peas sized hail. Wind speed and gust estimate not reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, October 23, Montana was in the crosshairs for a strong early winter snow storm set to begin later that afternoon afternoon and continue through Saturday evening. A strong upper level disturbance positioned over southeast Alaska would continue to move south-southeastward during the day, reaching the Pacific Northwest/British Columbia border by late in the afternoon. This feature would then approach western Montana overnight in conjunction with a strong surface cold front that would sweep southward out of Alberta into north central portions of the state. Widespread snow (heavy at times), blowing snow, and very cold wind chills were anticipated overnight and through the day on Saturday. Snow gradually tapered off Saturday night into early Sunday morning as the core of the upper level trough continued moving southward. Models were in excellent agreement on the details of this storm with very good run-to-run consistency so confidence was very high that impacts will be extensive and widespread. It appeared that the east slopes of the Rocky Mountains and much of central Montana would see upwards of 8 to 12 inches of snow accumulation, and some models even suggested that up to 18 inches of snow was not out of the question. The Winter Storm Warning remained in effect for this region, and was later upgraded to a Blizzard Warning for areas along the Rocky Mountain Front. Lesser amounts of snow were expected along eastern portions of the Hi-Line and in southwest Montana, so these areas were under a Winter Weather Advisory. Finally, a surface high pressure system would be centered over central and western Montana Sunday morning. This resulted in low temperatures on Sunday falling to near or below zero with many record temperatures expected to be set across the entire forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall of 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front shifted east across the region during the afternoon of October 30, bringing gusty winds along and behind it. Wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were most widespread across north-central MT through the late afternoon. Gusts to 40 mph were likely across much of SW MT as well going into the evening with the strongest gusts through mountain passes.||While winds began to decrease during the evening, strong wind gusts lingered into the overnight period across eastern portions of north-central MT before winds aloft and the surface pressure gradient there relaxed. Favorable conditions for mountain wave wind enhancement were present along the immediate east slopes of the Rockies early in the evening, where some localized gusts to around 80 mph occurred, particularly west of Choteau and Bynum.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station dw4821, chester reports wind gust of 63 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold, arctic air in place and the moisture from a Pacific low pressure system that tracked south and east of western Montana, created the perfect setup for heavy snow across western Montana. Widespread snowfall totals of near a foot were reported. The Potomac COOP reported a snowfall total of 18 inches on the 23rd, which is the highest, all-time 24 hour record since records began in 1964. Multiple stations reported their all-time coldest temperatures for the month of October few days later: 29 below zero in Potomac, 10 below at Seeley Lake and 7 below zero at Lindbergh Lake and Missoula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snowfall fell in the Mission Valley with reports of strong gusty winds causing blowing and drifting issues around Flathead Lake. The peak wind at the Flathead Lake Bio station was 46 mph at 4 am, and around that same time a large tree fell over blocking highway 35 near milepost 20. Snow amounts in inches include: 14 Hot Springs, 13 Arlee, 12 St. Ignatius, 11 located 8 miles south of Wood's Bay, 10.5 Ronan, and 5 in Kalispell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Complex upper level pattern across the western half of the CONUS, with broad troughing up and down the Pacific Coast and ridging extending north from the Central Plains into Northern Alberta/Saskatchewan. Within the base of the trough axis a robust |shortwave quickly lifts north northeastward from the eastern Great Basin/Central Rockies. This shortwave continues to quickly lift northeastward to over Eastern Montana. All the while an area of low pressure develops over southeast Montana and retrogrades northwest towards the Montana/Alberta/Saskatchewan borders by Sunday morning. This allows for numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop and lift northeast across the CWA through the remainder of the afternoon and into the morning hours on Sunday. Anomalously high precipitable water, characterized by values of 0.75 to 1.25,  continue to stream northward within southerly mid- to upper level flow through the night, allowing showers/storms to be efficient rain producers. Daytime heating and surface dewpoints in the mid 40s to mid 50s allow the atmosphere to become marginally unstable, which combined with 0-6km bulk shear of 40- 50kts continues to support the potential for at least some isolated severe thunderstorms through the evening hours despite widespread cloud cover. The biggest threat from any elevated storm over this time frame is large hail approaching half dollar size; however, can't rule out some damaging wind gusts of 60-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree blown down on Madison/Gilbert Street in Helena. Wind speed and gust estimate not reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer reported a whopping 18 inches of total storm snowfall on the 24th. The Seeley Lake COOP reported 11 inches on the 26th which makes it the highest 24 hour amount, topping the 9.6 inches observed on Oct. 31, 1942. Other snowfall amounts in inches include: 18 near Garnet Ghost Town, 17 Placid Lake, 15 five miles NW Seeley Lake, 14.3 Seeley Lake, and 10.9 Clinton.

The biggest threat from any elevated storm over this time frame is large hail approaching half dollar size; however, can't rule out some damaging wind gusts of 60-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Metal fence panels blown over at Catamount Street and Blackbird Drive. Branches broken in the city of Bozeman. Wind speed and gust estimate not reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Complex upper level pattern across the western half of the CONUS, with broad troughing up and down the Pacific Coast and ridging extending north from the Central Plains into Northern Alberta/Saskatchewan. Within the base of the trough axis a robust |shortwave quickly lifts north northeastward from the eastern Great Basin/Central Rockies. This shortwave continues to quickly lift northeastward to over Eastern Montana. All the while an area of low pressure develops over southeast Montana and retrogrades northwest towards the Montana/Alberta/Saskatchewan borders by Sunday morning. This allows for numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop and lift northeast across the CWA through the remainder of the afternoon and into the morning hours on Sunday. Anomalously high precipitable water, characterized by values of 0.75 to 1.25,  continue to stream northward within southerly mid- to upper level flow through the night, allowing showers/storms to be efficient rain producers. Daytime heating and surface dewpoints in the mid 40s to mid 50s allow the atmosphere to become marginally unstable, which combined with 0-6km bulk shear of 40- 50kts continues to support the potential for at least some isolated severe thunderstorms through the evening hours despite widespread cloud cover. The biggest threat from any elevated storm over this time frame is large hail approaching half dollar size; however, can't rule out some damaging wind gusts of 60-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Non-Thunderstorm wind gust report from Belgrade. ASOS station KBZN Bozeman Yellowstone airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front shifted east across the region during the afternoon of October 30, bringing gusty winds along and behind it. Wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were most widespread across north-central MT through the late afternoon. Gusts to 40 mph were likely across much of SW MT as well going into the evening with the strongest gusts through mountain passes.||While winds began to decrease during the evening, strong wind gusts lingered into the overnight period across eastern portions of north-central MT before winds aloft and the surface pressure gradient there relaxed. Favorable conditions for mountain wave wind enhancement were present along the immediate east slopes of the Rockies early in the evening, where some localized gusts to around 80 mph occurred, particularly west of Choteau and Bynum.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station dw9605, galata reports wind gust of 58 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another early season arctic airmass moved into the Billings Forecast Area toward the end of the month. At the same time, a strong upper level disturbance combined with moisture off the Pacific resulted in an overrunning event across south central Montana. The combination of these factors brought another round of heavy, wet snow to a large portion of the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 13 inches was reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow ahead of an approaching trough and attendant cold front continues to bring gusty winds and critical fire weather for much of the region through the evening. Wind gusts are mostly in the 30 to 40 mph range, with some higher gusts over southwest MT, where the strongest winds occur. Some of the mountain peaks and the north to south orientated valleys of the southwest have the potential for wind gusts up to 55 mph at times during the frontal passage, especially when showers are present. The approaching mid-level trough and surface low deepens across southern Alberta after crossing The Rockies with strong westerly flow across MT maintaining very windy conditions through Sunday. Potential for westerly wind gusts in excess of 50-60 mph |exists for much of north-central MT Sunday morning as westerly flow peaks south of the emerging trough in Alberta. The High Wind Warning for areas along The Rocky Mountain Front including The Cut Bank area remains in effect from midnight to 6 PM Sunday evening and the High Wind Watch for the remainder of north-central MT is upgraded to a High Wind Warning, which runs from 3 AM to 7 PM Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured gust of 50 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Friday, October 23, Montana was in the crosshairs for a strong early winter snow storm set to begin later that afternoon afternoon and continue through Saturday evening. A strong upper level disturbance positioned over southeast Alaska would continue to move south-southeastward during the day, reaching the Pacific Northwest/British Columbia border by late in the afternoon. This feature would then approach western Montana overnight in conjunction with a strong surface cold front that would sweep southward out of Alberta into north central portions of the state. Widespread snow (heavy at times), blowing snow, and very cold wind chills were anticipated overnight and through the day on Saturday. Snow gradually tapered off Saturday night into early Sunday morning as the core of the upper level trough continued moving southward. Models were in excellent agreement on the details of this storm with very good run-to-run consistency so confidence was very high that impacts will be extensive and widespread. It appeared that the east slopes of the Rocky Mountains and much of central Montana would see upwards of 8 to 12 inches of snow accumulation, and some models even suggested that up to 18 inches of snow was not out of the question. The Winter Storm Warning remained in effect for this region, and was later upgraded to a Blizzard Warning for areas along the Rocky Mountain Front. Lesser amounts of snow were expected along eastern portions of the Hi-Line and in southwest Montana, so these areas were under a Winter Weather Advisory. Finally, a surface high pressure system would be centered over central and western Montana Sunday morning. This resulted in low temperatures on Sunday falling to near or below zero with many record temperatures expected to be set across the entire forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter best guess is 6in. blowing/drifting; some bare spots, some drifts to 2ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of cold, arctic air in place and the moisture from a Pacific low pressure system that tracked south and east of western Montana, created the perfect setup for heavy snow across western Montana. Widespread snowfall totals of near a foot were reported. The Potomac COOP reported a snowfall total of 18 inches on the 23rd, which is the highest, all-time 24 hour record since records began in 1964. Multiple stations reported their all-time coldest temperatures for the month of October few days later: 29 below zero in Potomac, 10 below at Seeley Lake and 7 below zero at Lindbergh Lake and Missoula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow combined with gusty easterly winds ranging between 15 and 35 mph caused blowing and drifting snow from Ovando, south along the Continental Divide towards Deer Lodge. Sixteen and a half inches fell at Avon, and this is not far below nearby Elliston's two day record of 18.9 inches that occurred Oct. 4-5, 1914 (Elliston did not submit an observation for this 2020 event). Additional snow totals in inches include: 16 Edwards Gulch near Drummond, 15 MacDonald Pass, 14.5 three miles SW Ovando, 11 Drummond, 8 Georgetown Lake, 6 Anaconda and seven miles south of Butte, and 5.8 Butte.
