EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 12 inches were common across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow aloft delivered gusty westerly surface winds, along with scattered showers and mountain snow along the Divide and over portions of Central/North-central Montana on Monday October 12th. In addition, a few of these showers produced some lightning strikes along with moderate downpours of rain. This activity was expected to continue into the evening hours, before diminishing with the loss of daytime heating. The High Wind warning was continued due to sustained winds and gusts remained within striking distance of criteria values. There was a brief period of quiet weather as |transient ridging quickly traversed the region Monday night. The first sign of|the next weather maker was felt along the Divide Tuesday morning as a brief period of accumulating snow developed for locations above 5000 ft. The Winter Weather Advisory still looked good as of Monday afternoon as it appeared there could be some impacts to travel and outdoor interests before the warmer air moves in and inhibited snow accumulation. This area of precipitation then moved over the remainder of the CWA by Tuesday afternoon but snow levels were expected to rise above 7000 ft by afternoon. ||The focus then began to shift toward the potential strong winds from Tuesday afternoon into Wednesday morning as a wide swath of very strong upper level winds move into North-central and Southwest Montana. There was still some uncertainty on Monday whether these winds would translate to the surface wind gusts in excess of 60 mph, but the possibility looked best over the higher terrain. The area of greatest concern was areas south and west of a Lewistown, to Great Falls, to Cut Bank line where the strongest H700 winds of 50-70kts were expected to reside through Wednesday morning; which had the potential to make travel over mountain passes like MacDonald, Bozeman, Norris Hill, and Deep Creek hazardous as this looked like an ideal setup for high winds to materialize given a strong west- to-east flow. Given the situation, the current High Wind Watch was continued. Late Tuesday night into Wednesday morning, snow levels began to fall once again as colder air filled in on the back side of the system. This is when some lower elevation locations had the potential to see some light |accumulating snow over portions of North-central Montana, in addition to locations along the divide and the Central Mountains. Snowfall accumulations of 3-6 appeared possible along the Continental Divide and in the mountains of Central and North |Central Montana during the day on Wednesday, with even a dusting to up upwards of 2 over portions of the plains. especially along the Montana Hwy 200 corridor. A Winter Weather Advisory was issued on Tuesday for this snow. In addition to precipitation, another bout of high wind criteria wind speeds/gusts occurred during the morning and early afternoon hours across nearly the entire CWA (save for the Hi-Line east of Shelby) on Wednesday. Some mountain snow did occur Wednesday night that was reported early Thursday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station yctim 6.3 w big sky (avalanche).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With respect to wind, south to north orientated H700 jet max of 35-50kts/H500 jet max of 50-75kts remains over southwest and into central Montana through 03-06z Sunday before sliding eastward over The Northern High Plains. Deep diurnal mixing combined with terrain funneling, most notably in the north-south orientated valleys, continues to lead to sustained winds of 35-45 mph and gusts approaching 65 mph across southwest and into portions of central Montana. High Wind Warning continues for Beaverhead, |Madison, Gallatin, Jefferson, and Broadwater counties. While a few isolated wind gusts above High Wind criteria occur north of the High Wind Warning in Central Montana (Porphyry Peak in the Little Belt Mountains and Judith Peak in the Judith Mountains), have held off on expanding the High Wind Warning further north given the isolated nature of the gusts. With that being said, concern is that the H700/500 jet max slides further north between 21z Saturday and 03z Sunday that locations like Great Falls, Belt, and Stanford could briefly reach High Wind criteria. ||With respect to the potential for severe weather, the Storm Prediction Center continues to maintain a slight risk for portions of northwest Montana, mainly northwest of a line from Augusta, to Choteau, to Conrad, to Cut Bank. Afternoon storms are on-going across western Montana, with additional development expected along The Continental Divide through 00z Sunday. Hi-Res models also indicate the potential for storms to develop over far eastern portions of central and north central Montana before lifting north/northeast into southern Canada, but confidence in coverage and intensity of these storms is not as high as across northwest Montana. Large hail up to the size |of half dollar and damaging winds up to 60 mph are the primary threats with any severe thunderstorm, especially across the Slight Risk area. In addition to the threat for |severe weather, the potential for locally heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding also exists, especially where storms train over complex terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter emailed a picture. Also, reported 0.25 inches of rain. Time estimated based upon radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A west to east moving wave from Washington State, initiated a few supercell thunderstorms. These storms brought damaging hail and damaging wind to Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo on social media showed several hailstones that were a little larger than a quarter. Also hail was reported to have accumulated several inches in the storm's path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening surface low positioned over southern Alberta on October 16th generated strong southwest winds winds across the region during the day on Friday. The strongest winds were along the Rocky Mountain Front. As of 3 PM, short-term model guidance was supporting the expectation that winds would spread onto the adjacent plains and across North-central Montana late in the afternoon into the evening, once the surface low began to pull away from the Rocky Mountain Front. Thus, high wind warnings remained in effect at least through early evening.||Areas of light rain and mountain snow were ongoing Friday afternoon. These were associated with weak warm advection aloft, mainly for areas mainly north and east of|a line from Great Falls to Cut Bank. This activity appeared likely to continue through the evening, along with continuing snow over the Northern Rockies. ||As the surface low moved away to the east, the cold front would be drawn southward into North-central Montana. This was expected to occur by around 9pm for the HiLine, midnight for central MT, and early Saturday morning for south-central Montana. As the front moved by, light rain developed, which then changed over to snow. Initially wet roads were expected to freeze by morning, especially the bridges and overpasses. Wet snow sticking and freezing to power lines and branches had the potential to cause some isolated power outages. Rain and snowfall amounts were expected to be light across the North-central plains as the shallow front slid by. Some orographic enhancement in the northwest flow aloft had the potential to help maintain precipitation into Saturday for upslope areas along the Rocky Mountain Front and the upslope areas of central Montana. The cold air behind the front was shallow, and given northwesterly flow aloft, model guidance suggested the front would stall out somewhere north of I-90. Although this was not explicitly forecast at the time time, we would need to monitor for the possibility of the development of snow bands along this frontal convergence zone early|Saturday morning. Total snowfall amounts from this first wave had trended lower in Friday's updated forecast guidance. However, the forecaster noted that regardless of snow totals, any icing that developed with this early season event would be worth noting and extra precautions should be taken while driving.||The first set of Winter Weather Advisories remained in effect until 6 pm Saturday for north-central areas and midnight Saturday night for the southwest. A second disturbance would allow very little time to pass before the next round of snow and perhaps some mixed precipitation moved in late Saturday night and into Sunday. This system was a bit warmer but it it was a tricky forecast to know how much cold air would remain in the lower levels. There was potential that snow may become mixed with rain or even freezing drizzle at times, which will ultimately determine how much snow accumulation is realized. Most of this precipitation was expected to fall Sunday morning and afternoon but there was potential for some to linger through Monday morning. This resulted in another set of Winter Weather Advisories being issued from midnight Saturday through noon on Monday, for roughly the same areas as the first set that had been issued. Confidence was lowest in the Southwest due to the fact that warmer air may infiltrate before the most significant precipitation fell, but the forecaster decided to keep them in it since a little shift in timing could produce more impactful results. As mentioned earlier, forecast confidence in snowfall totals remain low but areas along the Divide and the central mountains had the potential to receive snow totals up to 6 inches or more in some cases. High temperatures for this afternoon were to be the coldest of the season thus far, with highs struggling to reach the 30s over much of Central/North-central Montana, with warmer 30s and 40s over the southwest. The cold temperatures would continue on Sunday but were expected to be slightly warmer than Saturday. More seasonal temperatures were expected by Monday afternoon after a brief period of patchy freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station mtpnd, pendroy (mt dot).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stronger than normal downslope winds developed behind a cold front passage. Much of the damage reported consisted of fallen trees and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread, downed trees over power lines were reported by a local utility across the Flathead Valley. West Valley on the west side Kalispell was especially hit hard. At 7 p.m. up to 53,000 members were out of power. A personal wind sensor in Kila measured a gust to 57 mph. In Hot Springs, the RAWS sensor reported a gust to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stronger than normal downslope winds developed behind a cold front passage. Much of the damage reported consisted of fallen trees and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees toppled from the winds in the Missoula area. In one case, the tree fell onto a parked car on 3rd Street. In Missoula, up to 1,900 people were without power for a period of time.  Fallen trees were also reported in the Bitterroot Valley, especially in Hamilton and  in Stevensville. Approximately 1,200 people lost power in the Bitterroot Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stronger than normal downslope winds developed behind a cold front passage. Much of the damage reported consisted of fallen trees and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting up to 75 mph were observed in the higher terrain across west-central Montana. While no damage was reported, this was considered a high impact event due to the fact that hunting season occurring and the winds could have caused tree-fall in the backcountry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough, moved through northwest Montana and created heavy snow bands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow bands, observed on radar from Lookout Pass east along I-90, brought snow rates high enough to reduce visibility and cause rapidly changing road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper-level trough moved across northwest Montana. Favorable conditions for snow bands developed with two distinct periods of intense snowfall: the first on the evening of the 10th, and the second just before morning rush hour on the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow rates of 1 to 2 inches an hour developed by 8:30 pm. Heavy snowfall amounts fell around the Missoula Valley, with at least 1 weather-related accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper-level trough moved across northwest Montana. Favorable conditions for snow bands developed with two distinct periods of intense snowfall: the first on the evening of the 10th, and the second just before morning rush hour on the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were observed. Additionally, total accumulations of snowfall were reported as 10.5 inches in Clinton and 9.2 inches in Bonner MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Arctic front pushed across the Continental Divide and brought heavy snow, blowing and drifting snow at most of the mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions were declared for Homestake Pass, from Avon to MacDonald Pass along US-12, and Avon to Nevada Lake along HWY-141 at 11 pm on the 7th. Chains were required for towing units on Homestake Pass at 11:45 pm on the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough and associated strong flow aloft brought strong surface winds to much of North-central and Southwest Montana on November 13th and 14th 2020. Surface winds strengthened well ahead of the approaching system as a tight pressure gradient developed between lee side troughing over the Canadian prairies and high pressure over Eastern Idaho and Wyoming. Once the trough axis passed, cold air advection helped bring the 50 to 70 kt H700 winds to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jeffrey station (5 SSE Stockett) measured a 56 kt (65 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system moving slowly over the region pulled in very gusty surface winds from the east and southeast ahead of the main circulation.  Any passing rain shower provided an easy path for stronger winds aloft to be pulled down to the surface, especially within 20 to 30 miles of the Montana-North Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site (MTBVR), sustained winds of at least 40 mph were recorded at least each hour through most of the early morning, with a peak wind gust of 61 mph recorded from 7:31 to 7:38 MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site (MTBVR), sustained winds of at least 40 mph were recorded at least each hour through the afternoon on the 6th, with a peak wind gust of 58 mph recorded multiple times between 4:00 PM and 6:00 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale closed upper level low slowly migrates across the Northwestern U.S. through Thursday. As such, continue to experience below average temperatures and periods of widespread rain and mountain snow. Most precipitation spreads across |southwest into central Montana, developing on radar. Weak instability beneath a strongly divergent flow aloft enables a few embedded thunderstorms, capable of producing locally heavy rainfall rates and small hail. The 700 mb trough axis crosses the region overnight, helping to lower snow levels to around 5500 feet across southwest Montana. A combination of cold advection and dynamic cooling associated with the prolonged period of ascent brings snow levels below that indicated by model guidance. Thus, forecast snow accumulation and snow levels show some variation among the ensemble members, as well as variations in the location of heaviest precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures show cold air funnel 6 mi s Great Falls, looking south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of wrap-around precipitation developed within northwest flow, associated with a closed upper level low, positioned just north of Helena. This brought over a foot of heavy snow to locations above 4500 feet in southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread power outages, slick roads with low visibility, and extensive tree branch damage was reported. Total snow amounts included 16 inches in Walkersville, between 8 and 10 inches in Butte, and 8 inches in Deer Lodge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms develop across western and southwest Montana, and continue to become more widespread. Daytime heating provides moderate instability for convective initiation. Inverted-v type soundings, characterized by a deep and dry boundary layer, support DCAPE values near 1000 J/kg. Thus, damaging wind gusts are the primary hazard associated with stronger thunderstorms. Shear profiles strengthen and may be enough to support a few supercell thunderstorms capable of producing locally heavy |rain, small hail, and with marginal support for an isolated larger hail event to the size of quarters. The severe storm potential peaks by early evening, although showers and thunderstorms continue to spread across the region overnight. This activity is associated with a passing upper level shortwave trough and its associated surface front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lot of quarter to ping pong sized hail. Also, reports wind shredded pine trees and a lot of branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cross barrier flow developed over the Rocky Mountain Front and Central/North-central plains as high pressure ridging aloft was flattened by subtle Pacific disturbances. Tightening of the lower level pressure gradient was observed as a strong surface high remained anchored southwest of the Divide. These factors combined with mountain wave activity brought the final high wind event for November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cut Bank Airport measured a 62 kt (71 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very anomalous wind pattern set up over southwest Montana where 60 mph winds mixed down to the surface for multiple hours. In the last 40 years, the Butte area had never seen such a strong, pre-frontal wind event in terms of duration and peak intensities, not connected with thunderstorms or convective showers. This storm rubbed salt in the wound of a damaging, heavy snow storm that occurred five days previously. Citizens of Butte were still trying to clean-up after that event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds between 40 to 50 mph with frequent gusts between 50 and 63 mph occurred during the event. Uptown Butte sustained the most roof damage with blown-off shingles, partially due to the older style homes found there. Multiple trees and power lines were damaged which resulted in power outages. Isolated reports of damaged trees and power lines were found from Anaconda to Deer Lodge. Downed power lines caused two separate fires: a quarter acre grass fire located four miles south-southwest of Butte, and a two acre grass and timber fire southwest of Homestake Pass along Blacktail Canyon Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds from a thunderstorm damaged power lines and brought power outages to the west Hamilton area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cross barrier flow developed over the Rocky Mountain Front and Central/North-central plains as high pressure ridging aloft was flattened by subtle Pacific disturbances. Tightening of the lower level pressure gradient was observed as a strong surface high remained anchored southwest of the Divide. These factors combined with mountain wave activity brought the final high wind event for November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station at 8 ESE Fort Benton measured a 51 kt (59 mph) non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A west to east moving wave from Washington State, initiated a few supercell thunderstorms. These storms brought damaging hail and damaging wind to Lincoln County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two main areas of thunderstorms developing during the early afternoon hours: 1) Hi-Line due to convergence zone location (southwest and northeast surface winds), and 2) southwest/central Montana mountains due to orographic lift/terrain heating. As the |afternoon progresses and instability continues to build, thunderstorms continue to increase in areal coverage and intensity, especially across portions of southwest and central Montana where wave/left exit region of an upper level jet moves across and promotes additional lift. The threat for severe weather is maximized during the afternoon and early evening across Southwest and Central Montana where 0-6km bulk shear of 45-65kts and MLCAPE approaching 1500J/kg is found. The SPC continues to maintain a slight risk for severe thunderstorms south of a line from Helena, to Raynesford, to Winifred and generally north of the I-90 corridor; with a marginal |risk for the remainder of the CWA southeast of a Choteau to Havre line. Thunderstorms that become severe could produce large hail to the size of half dollars and wind gusts to 60 mph. In addition, these thunderstorms are  capable of producing torrential rainfall given PWATs approaching the 90% moving average.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and hail of at least one inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Recent rainfall combined with melting snow raises river levels for some central and southern rivers. Overall limited impacts expected; however, The Big Hole River near Melrose experiences a little more upstream water flowing into it. Water levels rise above minor flood stage, and are expected to crest the evening of Friday June 19th, near 6.5 ft,|before slowly receding below flood stage late Sunday evening. As a result, the Flood Warning for the Big Hole River near Melrose remains in affect through Sunday night, and anyone near this areas should stay away from flooded roads and monitor any property near the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 8:30 PM MDT Friday the stage was 6.3 feet.|Flood stage is 6.0 feet.|Minor flooding is occurring.|Impact...At 6.2 Feet on the Big Hole River...water starts to come out of its bank and affects a home/driveway near Glen. Look at staff gauge GNLM8...that should be around 6.5 feet on the Big Hole River near Glen for flooding around Glen (June 2017).|Flood History...This crest compares to a previous crest of 6.3 feet on 05/21/1969.|At 3:30 AM MDT Saturday the stage was 5.9 feet.|Flood stage is 6.0 feet.|Forecast...The river is expected to continue to slowly fall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure at the surface combined with seasonally hot temperatures, an unstable atmosphere, and enough shear to generate and sustain strong thunderstorms across portions of eastern Montana - a few of which produced severe wind gusts in Valley and Dawson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Beaver Hill I-94 DOT site (MTBVR), the first severe wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at 11:02 AM MST.  Sustained winds speeds well above 40 mph and wind gusts well above 60 mph continued over the next 6 hours.  The peak wind gust of 71 mph was recorded at 12:32 PM MST.  The last severe wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at 5:43 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For this time of year, after a warmer day on the 9th, recent additional melting of the patchy snow pack and some wet ground around the region provided some additional moisture near the surface.  Early on the morning on the 10th, as temperatures dropped well into the teens, conditions were favorable for some areas of freezing fog development, which persisted off and on for a few days in this stagnant weather pattern.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Plentywood airport mesonet site (KPWD), visibility dropped to a quarter mile or less for nearly 12 hours from the night of the 10th through the morning of the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and steady jet stream stalled over the region. A few scattered showers and a favorable atmosphere allowed those stronger winds from aloft to reach down to the surface in southwest Phillips Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site (MTMAL), short peak wind gusts of 58 mph were recorded in the late morning and early afternoon on the 22nd.
